stem: A 34-year-old man is brought to a level I trauma center after an enclosed-space industrial explosion. He has no external penetrating wounds, but reports chest pain and dyspnea. Vitals: HR 118, BP 104/70, RR 30, SpO2 86% on a non-rebreather. Exam shows decreased breath sounds, scattered crackles, and tympanic-membrane rupture. Chest radiograph reveals a bilateral 'butterfly' perihilar infiltrate without rib fractures or pneumothorax. He requires intubation. Which management strategy is MOST appropriate for his primary injury?

choices.text: Administer aggressive crystalloid resuscitation (2 L bolus) to support the elevated heart rate and presumed hypovolemia
choices.rationale: Liberal crystalloid worsens blast lung injury by exacerbating pulmonary edema, so fluids should be judiciously restricted unless there is hemorrhagic shock.

choices.text: Initiate high tidal-volume (10-12 mL/kg) ventilation with high PEEP to rapidly recruit collapsed alveoli
choices.rationale: Large tidal volumes and high airway pressures promote alveolar overdistension, arterial gas embolism, and pneumothorax in already-injured blast lung.

choices.text: Use lung-protective low tidal-volume ventilation with permissive hypercapnia, the lowest effective FiO2/pressures, and judicious fluids
choices.rationale: Correct — primary blast (pulmonary) injury behaves like ARDS, so low tidal volumes, minimized airway pressure, and restricted fluids reduce barotrauma, air embolism, and edema.

choices.text: Place bilateral prophylactic chest tubes before any positive-pressure ventilation
choices.rationale: There is no current pneumothorax, and routine prophylactic tubes are not indicated; vigilance for barotrauma during ventilation is the appropriate approach.

choices.text: Transfuse two units of packed red cells empirically to treat the tachycardia and hypoxemia
choices.rationale: His hypoxemia is from pulmonary injury, not anemia, and there is no evidence of hemorrhage, so empiric transfusion is unwarranted and adds volume to injured lungs.

explanation: Primary blast injury results from the over-/under-pressurization wave acting on gas-containing organs (lung, GI tract, middle ear); tympanic-membrane rupture is a clue to significant blast exposure. Blast lung injury produces an ARDS-like picture, and positive-pressure ventilation carries a high risk of pneumothorax and systemic arterial air embolism. Management therefore mirrors lung-protective ARDS care: low tidal volumes, permissive hypercapnia, the lowest effective FiO2 and airway pressures, and restrictive fluid management while watching closely for barotrauma.
subtopic: Primary blast (pulmonary) injury — ventilation and resuscitation strategy

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The stem is a textbook primary blast lung injury (BLI): enclosed-space explosion (worst case for primary blast), no penetrating trauma, hypoxemia, TM rupture (marker of significant overpressure), and a bilateral perihilar "butterfly" infiltrate without rib fractures or pneumothorax. The keyed answer (index 2) — lung-protective low tidal-volume ventilation, permissive hypercapnia, lowest effective FiO2/pressures, judicious fluids — is the single best answer and reflects current consensus (CDC/military/trauma literature): BLI behaves like ARDS, positive-pressure ventilation carries high risk of pneumothorax and systemic arterial air embolism, and fluid overload worsens pulmonary edema. All distractors are genuinely inferior: (A) 2 L crystalloid bolus worsens BLI edema absent hemorrhagic shock; (B) high TV 10-12 mL/kg + high PEEP causes overdistension/barotrauma/air embolism; (D) bilateral prophylactic chest tubes are not indicated with no pneumothorax and constitute overtreatment; (E) empiric PRBC transfusion is unwarranted with no evidence of hemorrhage and adds volume to injured lungs. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current. Only a minor unstated nuance (permissive hypercapnia should be tempered if cerebral air embolism/raised ICP is a concern) — a refinement, not an error, and does not unseat the keyed answer. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430914/ resolves to StatPearls "Blast Injuries," which is genuinely on-topic — it covers primary blast injury/barotrauma, blast lung recognition (dyspnea/cough/hemoptysis/chest pain), and overpressure-related pneumothorax and air embolism. Citation is valid and relevant. Both checks pass.

stem: A previously healthy 70-kg, 28-year-old woman arrives after a high-speed motor-vehicle collision with a closed femur fracture and abdominal tenderness. She is anxious and confused. Vitals: HR 122, BP 92/78 (narrowed pulse pressure), RR 30, and urine output 18 mL over the last hour. She has cool, mottled extremities and a capillary refill of 4 seconds; arterial base deficit is -8 mmol/L. Based on the ATLS classification of hemorrhage, approximately how much circulating blood volume has she most likely lost, and what is the appropriate next step?

choices.text: Class I (<15%, <750 mL); reassure and observe with maintenance fluids only
choices.rationale: Class I hemorrhage shows essentially normal vital signs and mentation, which does not fit her tachycardia, narrowed pulse pressure, confusion, and oliguria.

choices.text: Class III (30-40%, ~1500-2000 mL); begin balanced resuscitation and activate the massive transfusion protocol while controlling the bleeding source
choices.rationale: Correct — tachycardia >120, narrowed pulse pressure, confusion, oliguria, and a rising base deficit are hallmarks of Class III hemorrhage, which warrants blood-product resuscitation and source control rather than crystalloid alone.

choices.text: Class II (15-30%, ~750-1500 mL); 2 L of crystalloid is expected to fully restore her hemodynamics
choices.rationale: Class II typically preserves mentation and systolic pressure with only mild anxiety, whereas her confusion and oliguria indicate she has progressed beyond Class II.

choices.text: Class IV (>40%, >2000 mL); she should already be pulseless and is in cardiac arrest
choices.rationale: Class IV produces profound hypotension and obtundation but not necessarily arrest, and her palpable pressure and responsiveness place her in Class III rather than Class IV.

choices.text: Hemorrhage class cannot be estimated without a hemoglobin level, so resuscitation should wait for laboratory results
choices.rationale: Acute hemoglobin lags behind volume loss and is unreliable early, so the ATLS class is estimated clinically and resuscitation must not be delayed for labs.

explanation: The ATLS hemorrhage classes correlate clinical signs with estimated blood loss in a healthy 70-kg adult: Class I (<15%), Class II (15-30%, tachycardia with narrowed pulse pressure), Class III (30-40%, marked tachycardia/hypotension, confusion, oliguria), and Class IV (>40%, life-threatening). A narrowed pulse pressure appears early because rising catecholamines raise the diastolic pressure, and altered mentation with a worsening base deficit signals significant loss. Class III hemorrhage calls for balanced blood-product resuscitation and rapid source control; relying on crystalloid alone or waiting for a hemoglobin result delays definitive care.
subtopic: ATLS hemorrhage classification and shock recognition

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (Class III, 30-40%, ~1500-2000 mL) is the single best answer and is unambiguous. The stem's constellation anchors firmly in Class III per ATLS: HR 122 (>120, the Class III threshold), altered mentation/confusion (Class III hallmark, absent in Class I/II), systolic hypotension (92), oliguria (~18 mL/hr indicating significant loss), and base deficit -8 (ATLS 10th-ed base-deficit stratification places -6 to -10 in Class III). Distractors are all genuinely inferior: Class I requires near-normal vitals/mentation (excluded); Class II preserves mentation/SBP (her confusion places her beyond it); Class IV is correctly excluded — the distractor's claim that she 'should already be pulseless and in cardiac arrest' is a deliberate falsehood (Class IV is profound shock, not arrest), and she is responsive with a palpable pressure; the 'wait for hemoglobin' option is correctly wrong because acute Hgb lags volume loss. The narrowed pulse pressure (92/78 = 14 mmHg) is consistent with the explanation's accurate catecholamine/diastolic mechanism; very narrow PP is emphasized at Class IV but is not exclusionary for Class III, and every other parameter lands in Class III. Next-step management (balanced blood-product resuscitation, MTP activation, source control rather than crystalloid alone) reflects current damage-control resuscitation doctrine. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470382/ resolves to StatPearls 'Hemorrhagic Shock,' which explicitly details the ATLS Class I-IV classification with matching blood-loss percentages, volumes, HR ranges, pulse pressure, and mental-status changes — directly on-topic and not fabricated.

stem: A 19-year-old man is pulled from an icy lake after an estimated 25 minutes of submersion. On EMS arrival he is apneic and pulseless; an esophageal probe reads a core temperature of 28°C (82°F). CPR is in progress, and the cardiac monitor shows ventricular fibrillation. He is intubated and an IO line is placed. Which statement best guides his resuscitation?

choices.text: Because the down-time exceeds 20 minutes, resuscitation is futile and efforts should be stopped after the first rhythm check
choices.rationale: Severe hypothermia is neuroprotective in cold-water drowning, so prolonged resuscitation can yield intact survival and efforts should not be abandoned based on down-time alone.

choices.text: Repeated 1-mg epinephrine doses should be given every 3-5 minutes throughout, exactly as in a normothermic arrest
choices.rationale: At core temperatures below ~30°C the myocardium responds poorly to drugs and they accumulate, so ACLS recommends withholding or spacing vasopressors until rewarming progresses.

choices.text: Defibrillation should be deferred entirely until the core temperature reaches 36°C
choices.rationale: A single defibrillation attempt is reasonable for VF in severe hypothermia, and further shocks are limited (not indefinitely deferred) until the patient is rewarmed above ~30°C.

choices.text: Continue high-quality CPR and active rewarming (ideally extracorporeal), limiting shocks/drugs until core temperature rises, because the patient is 'not dead until warm and dead'
choices.rationale: Correct — in hypothermic drowning arrest, CPR and aggressive core rewarming (extracorporeal membrane oxygenation/bypass when available) continue with limited shocks and vasopressors until the core temperature is restored before declaring death.

choices.text: Rapid surface rewarming of the limbs with hot packs is the priority to quickly normalize temperature
choices.rationale: Peripheral/surface rewarming risks core-temperature afterdrop and vasodilatory collapse, so core rewarming methods are preferred over isolated external limb warming.

explanation: Cold-water submersion can produce profound hypothermia that lowers cerebral metabolic demand and confers neuroprotection, so good neurologic recovery is possible even after prolonged arrest — hence the maxim 'not dead until warm and dead.' Below roughly 30°C the hypothermic heart is refractory to defibrillation and vasoactive drugs, which also accumulate; guidelines therefore limit repeat shocks and space or withhold epinephrine until rewarming progresses. The priority is high-quality CPR with active core rewarming, with extracorporeal rewarming (ECMO/cardiopulmonary bypass) being the most effective method when available.
subtopic: Cold-water drowning with hypothermic cardiac arrest — resuscitation and rewarming

verdict.reason:
CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. A 19-year-old in icy-water submersion arrest with core temp 28°C (severe hypothermia, <30°C) and VF is the textbook scenario for "not dead until warm and dead." Keyed answer D (continue high-quality CPR + active core rewarming, ideally extracorporeal/ECMO-CPB, limiting repeat shocks and vasopressors until rewarming) matches current AHA/ERC guidance and is clearly the single best answer. Distractors are genuinely inferior and their rationales are accurate: A (down-time futility) wrongly ignores hypothermic neuroprotection; B (q3-5min epi as in normothermia) is wrong because vasopressors are withheld/spaced below ~30°C due to reduced metabolism/accumulation; C (defer ALL defibrillation until 36°C) is wrong because a single shock attempt is reasonable in severe hypothermic VF; E (surface limb rewarming with hot packs) risks afterdrop/vasodilatory collapse and core rewarming is preferred. The explanation correctly states the ~30°C refractoriness threshold and ECMO/CPB as the most effective rewarming method. One trivial internal looseness (choice C stem says "36°C" while its rationale references ">~30°C") does not create ambiguity or affect the keyed answer.

REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430833/ resolves to the correct StatPearls page "Drowning: Clinical Management" (updated Sept 2025), genuinely on-topic. It covers drowning resuscitation, 30-second pulse checks in hypothermia, limited role of shockable rhythms (2-12%), and explicitly notes ECMO may be considered for severe hypothermia. It is somewhat general (does not name the "not dead until warm and dead" maxim or give granular sub-30°C epi protocols), but it is a valid, relevant primary citation for this drowning-with-hypothermia question. No reference problem.

stem: A 68-year-old man (BMI 24) with COPD undergoes a right upper lobectomy via thoracotomy. A 39-Fr left-sided double-lumen tube is placed and confirmed in position by fiberoptic bronchoscopy. Shortly after one-lung ventilation (left lung) begins with FiO2 0.8, his SpO2 drifts from 99% to 84% over 4 minutes. Peak airway pressure is 24 cmH2O, blood pressure 118/70 mmHg, heart rate 78 bpm, and end-tidal CO2 38 mmHg. The surgeon has just opened the pleura and has not yet begun resection. After increasing FiO2 to 1.0, which is the single most appropriate NEXT step?

choices.text: Reconfirm double-lumen tube position with the fiberoptic bronchoscope
choices.rationale: Tube malposition (the most common reversible cause of OLV hypoxemia) must be re-excluded with the bronchoscope before escalating to ventilatory maneuvers, even when an earlier check looked correct, because the tube can migrate after patient positioning.

choices.text: Apply 5 cmH2O CPAP with oxygen to the nondependent (operative, right) lung
choices.rationale: CPAP to the nondependent lung is an effective rescue but is reserved for after tube position is confirmed and recruitment/PEEP fail, and it would partially re-inflate the operative field the surgeon needs collapsed.

choices.text: Add 10 cmH2O PEEP to the dependent (ventilated, left) lung immediately
choices.rationale: Dependent-lung PEEP can reduce atelectasis but should follow confirmation of correct tube position, and excessive PEEP may divert pulmonary blood flow to the nonventilated lung and worsen shunt.

choices.text: Clamp the pulmonary artery of the operative lung to abolish the shunt
choices.rationale: Although PA ligation eliminates shunt, it is a surgical maneuver performed during resection and is inappropriate as an immediate response to acute desaturation early in the case.

choices.text: Resume two-lung ventilation and abandon lung isolation for the procedure
choices.rationale: Resuming two-lung ventilation is the fallback for refractory hypoxemia, not the first step when a readily correctable cause such as tube malposition has not yet been excluded.

explanation: When SpO2 falls during one-lung ventilation, the structured response is to raise FiO2 toward 1.0 and then immediately re-verify double-lumen tube position with the fiberoptic bronchoscope, because tube migration after lateral positioning is the most common and most rapidly correctable cause of hypoxemia. Only after malposition is excluded do you proceed to recruitment of the dependent lung, dependent-lung PEEP, and CPAP to the nondependent lung. PA clamping and abandoning isolation are later or surgical measures reserved for confirmed refractory hypoxemia.
subtopic: Thoracic anesthesia: management of hypoxemia during one-lung ventilation

verdict.reason: Reference (OpenAnesthesia "Hypoxia During One-Lung Ventilation") resolves and is directly on-topic, explicitly covering the stepwise approach: raise FiO2, fiberoptic confirmation of DLT/blocker position, recruitment, CPAP to nondependent lung, PEEP to dependent lung. Clinically the keyed answer (reconfirm DLT position with the bronchoscope) is the single best NEXT step: DLT malposition is the most common rapidly-correctable cause of OLV hypoxemia, and the tube can migrate during lateral positioning (the case moves from supine confirmation to lateral decubitus before OLV), so re-checking despite an earlier confirmation is standard. The patient is hemodynamically stable with a gradual drift (not a precipitous crash), which justifies a diagnostic step before escalating rescue maneuvers. All distractors are genuinely inferior and their rationales are accurate: CPAP to nondependent lung and dependent-lung PEEP are later ventilatory rescues (and PEEP can divert flow/worsen shunt); PA clamp is a surgical maneuver done during resection; resuming two-lung ventilation is the fallback for refractory hypoxemia. Only the correct choice is flagged rationale_correct=true; the others are correctly false. Answer is unambiguous and current.

stem: A 72-year-old woman with a 90% symptomatic left internal carotid stenosis, well-controlled hypertension, and type 2 diabetes presents for left carotid endarterectomy. The surgical team plans an awake technique under combined superficial and deep cervical plexus block specifically to allow direct neurologic monitoring and selective shunting. Her right hand is positioned on an arm board with a squeaky toy for grip testing. Approximately 45 seconds after the surgeon applies the carotid cross-clamp, the patient stops following commands, develops slurred speech, and her right-hand grip weakens. Blood pressure is 138/80 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm, and SpO2 99%. Which intervention is most appropriate?

choices.text: Deepen sedation with propofol and proceed, attributing the change to anxiety
choices.rationale: Sedating the patient would eliminate the very neurologic monitoring the awake technique provides and could mask ongoing cerebral ischemia.

choices.text: Convert immediately to general anesthesia and intubate before any other action
choices.rationale: Emergent conversion to general anesthesia abandons real-time neurologic feedback and delays the definitive fix for the ischemia, which is restoring flow via a shunt.

choices.text: Inform the surgeon so a carotid shunt can be placed to restore cerebral perfusion
choices.rationale: New focal deficits appearing after cross-clamp in an awake patient indicate inadequate collateral flow, and prompt placement of an intraluminal shunt restores perfusion and is the gold-standard response to a positive awake neurologic test.

choices.text: Administer a phenylephrine infusion to raise systolic pressure above 180 mmHg as definitive treatment
choices.rationale: Modestly augmenting blood pressure may improve collateral flow as a temporizing measure, but it is not definitive and should not substitute for shunting when a clear focal deficit has developed.

choices.text: Give a 500 mL crystalloid bolus and reassess the deficit in 5 minutes
choices.rationale: A fluid bolus does not address cross-clamp cerebral ischemia, and a 5-minute delay risks completed stroke when neurons are already failing.

explanation: Awake carotid endarterectomy under cervical plexus block is the most sensitive and specific method for detecting cross-clamp cerebral ischemia, allowing selective rather than routine shunting. A new contralateral motor deficit or change in mentation within the first minutes after clamping signals inadequate collateral flow through the circle of Willis, and the definitive response is to alert the surgeon to place an intraluminal shunt. Blood pressure augmentation is a reasonable adjunct while the shunt is being placed but is not the primary fix.
subtopic: Vascular anesthesia: awake carotid endarterectomy and selective shunting
reference_title: Carotid endarterectomy using regional anesthesia: technique and considerations

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. For an awake CEA under cervical plexus block, a new focal deficit (contralateral right-hand weakness, slurred speech, stops following commands) appearing ~45 sec after LEFT carotid cross-clamp is a positive awake neurologic test indicating inadequate collateral flow through the circle of Willis. Laterality is internally consistent (left CEA → left-hemisphere ischemia → contralateral/right motor deficit plus dysarthria/aphasia from the dominant hemisphere). The keyed answer (alert surgeon to place an intraluminal shunt) is the gold-standard definitive response because it restores cerebral perfusion. Distractors are all genuinely inferior: deepening sedation masks monitoring and is dangerous; converting to GA abandons real-time monitoring and delays flow restoration; the phenylephrine option is correctly wrong because it labels BP augmentation as "definitive treatment" (it is only an adjunct/temporizing measure while the shunt is placed); a fluid bolus plus 5-min reassessment dangerously delays definitive care. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current. No second defensible answer.\n\nREFERENCE: WebFetch of https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11187481/ resolves to "Carotid endarterectomy using regional anesthesia: technique and considerations" (Frontiers in Surgery, 2024). It is precisely on-topic: it covers superficial cervical plexus block, minimal sedation to preserve neuromonitoring, awake motor testing with a squeaky toy (the exact stem detail), and selective shunting triggered by changes in the neurologic exam. Both checks pass.

stem: A 64-year-old man with a 6.8-cm descending thoracic aortic aneurysm and a prior infrarenal aortic graft undergoes open repair through a left thoracotomy. A lumbar cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) drain is placed preoperatively as part of a spinal cord protection strategy. The aorta is cross-clamped just distal to the left subclavian artery (a high thoracic clamp). Just after clamping, the anesthesiologist notes the upper-body mean arterial pressure has risen sharply. The intensivist asks how the CSF drain protects the cord in this setting. Which statement best explains the rationale for maintaining CSF pressure at or below 10 mmHg during and after this repair?

choices.text: Lowering CSF pressure raises spinal cord perfusion pressure, which equals distal aortic (or collateral) pressure minus CSF pressure
choices.rationale: Spinal cord perfusion pressure is the difference between the cord's arterial inflow pressure and CSF pressure, so draining CSF to 10 mmHg or less directly increases net perfusion pressure when distal aortic flow is compromised by the clamp.

choices.text: Draining CSF directly dilates the artery of Adamkiewicz, increasing anterior spinal artery diameter
choices.rationale: CSF drainage has no direct vasodilatory effect on the artery of Adamkiewicz; it works by lowering the external pressure opposing cord perfusion, not by changing vessel caliber.

choices.text: CSF drainage lowers systemic venous pressure and thereby reduces cardiac afterload during cross-clamp
choices.rationale: CSF drainage does not meaningfully alter systemic venous pressure or ventricular afterload; afterload during a high clamp is managed pharmacologically, not by the lumbar drain.

choices.text: Removing CSF cools the spinal cord, providing the neuroprotection of deep hypothermia
choices.rationale: Lumbar CSF drainage does not produce regional spinal cord hypothermia; therapeutic cord cooling, when used, is achieved by epidural or systemic cooling techniques, not by drain volume removal.

choices.text: A high (proximal) thoracic clamp reliably reduces preload, so the drain is needed to offset the resulting hypotension
choices.rationale: A proximal thoracic cross-clamp characteristically raises proximal pressure and afterload rather than reducing preload, and the drain's purpose is to optimize cord perfusion pressure, not to counter clamp-induced hypotension.

explanation: Spinal cord perfusion pressure equals the cord's arterial inflow pressure minus CSF (intrathecal) pressure, so during thoracic aortic cross-clamping—when distal aortic and collateral inflow are reduced—draining CSF to 10 mmHg or less raises net perfusion pressure and lowers the risk of ischemic paraplegia. CSF drainage does not dilate the artery of Adamkiewicz, cool the cord, or alter systemic afterload. Note that a high (proximal) thoracic clamp increases proximal arterial pressure and afterload; meaningful preload augmentation from clamping occurs only with supraceliac clamps, where the splanchnic venous bed is excluded.
subtopic: Vascular anesthesia: spinal cord protection and CSF drainage in descending thoracic aortic repair
reference_title: Prevention of Spinal Cord Injury during Thoracoabdominal Aortic Aneurysms Repair: What the Anaesthesiologist Should Know

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer (index 0) is the single best answer. Spinal cord perfusion pressure = arterial inflow pressure − CSF pressure, so draining CSF to ≤10 mmHg raises net SCPP; this is the foundational physiology and is reinforced by the reference (SCPP = MAP − CSF pressure). The choice's phrasing "distal aortic (or collateral) pressure" is in fact more precise than MAP for the cross-clamp setting, since the cord below a high clamp is perfused via collateral/distal aortic flow. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and their rationales are accurate: CSF drainage does not dilate the artery of Adamkiewicz (1), does not alter systemic venous pressure/afterload (2), does not cool the cord (3), and a proximal thoracic clamp raises afterload rather than reducing preload (4). The explanation's nuance that meaningful preload augmentation occurs only with supraceliac clamps (splanchnic venous exclusion) is correct. Stem is internally consistent — the sharp rise in upper-body MAP after a high clamp matches increased proximal afterload and correctly contradicts distractor 4. No ambiguity. REFERENCE: reference_url (PMC9605294) resolves via standard NCBI 301 redirect to pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov; title matches exactly ("Prevention of Spinal Cord Injury during Thoracoabdominal Aortic Aneurysms Repair: What the Anaesthesiologist Should Know") and the page explicitly states SCPP = MAP − CSF pressure and recommends maintaining CSF pressure ≤10 mmHg — directly on-topic. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 9-year-old boy with a several-day history of progressive lower-extremity weakness presents for an emergent appendectomy. His parents report he was recently diagnosed with Guillain-Barré syndrome and has been wheelchair-bound for 2 weeks. He is now in respiratory distress with copious secretions, and the surgeon requests a rapid-sequence induction. Vitals: HR 118, BP 104/62, SpO2 94% on 6 L nasal cannula. The anesthesiologist plans induction with propofol and a neuromuscular blocking agent. Which agent is most likely to precipitate a life-threatening cardiac arrhythmia if administered to this patient?

choices.text: Succinylcholine 1.5 mg/kg IV
choices.rationale: Correct: demyelinating/denervating conditions such as Guillain-Barré upregulate extrajunctional acetylcholine receptors, so succinylcholine can trigger massive potassium efflux and hyperkalemic cardiac arrest.

choices.text: Rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg IV
choices.rationale: Incorrect: rocuronium is a nondepolarizing agent that does not cause depolarization-related potassium release and is the preferred RSI relaxant in this patient.

choices.text: Vecuronium 0.2 mg/kg IV
choices.rationale: Incorrect: vecuronium is a nondepolarizing aminosteroid that does not provoke hyperkalemia, though its onset is too slow for ideal RSI.

choices.text: Cisatracurium 0.2 mg/kg IV
choices.rationale: Incorrect: cisatracurium is a nondepolarizing benzylisoquinolinium that undergoes Hofmann elimination and carries no risk of hyperkalemic arrest.

choices.text: Propofol 3 mg/kg IV
choices.rationale: Incorrect: propofol may cause hypotension but does not trigger potassium-mediated arrhythmias related to receptor upregulation.

explanation: Succinylcholine normally raises serum potassium by about 0.5 mEq/L, but in conditions that upregulate extrajunctional (fetal-type) acetylcholine receptors—including denervating and demyelinating disorders such as Guillain-Barré syndrome, prolonged immobility, burns beyond 48 hours, and major denervation injury—depolarization can release enough potassium to cause hyperkalemic cardiac arrest. The risk persists as long as receptor upregulation is present, often well beyond the acute phase. A nondepolarizing agent such as rocuronium is the appropriate choice when rapid intubating conditions are needed in these patients.
subtopic: Neuromuscular blockers — succinylcholine and hyperkalemia in receptor upregulation

verdict.reason: Clinical content is correct and unambiguous. Guillain-Barré syndrome upregulates extrajunctional/fetal-type acetylcholine receptors, so succinylcholine causes exaggerated potassium efflux and can precipitate hyperkalemic cardiac arrest — the single best answer. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: rocuronium, vecuronium, and cisatracurium are nondepolarizing agents that do not cause depolarization-related hyperkalemia (rocuronium correctly identified as the preferred RSI relaxant), and propofol causes hypotension rather than a receptor-mediated hyperkalemic arrhythmia. The explanation (normal ~0.5 mEq/L rise, list of upregulating conditions, persistence of risk, nondepolarizing agent recommendation) is accurate and current. Dosing (succinylcholine 1.5 mg/kg RSI) is appropriate. The stem is internally consistent — both GBS and 2-week immobility independently support the same answer — with no competing defensible choice. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/succinylcholine/ resolves successfully and is directly on-topic, explicitly covering extrajunctional receptor upregulation, hyperkalemic cardiac arrest, and Guillain-Barré syndrome as a susceptibility condition. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 58-year-old woman with hypertension and major depressive disorder treated with fluoxetine presents for a parathyroidectomy. Intraoperatively, after the surgeon requests dye localization, 1 mg/kg of methylene blue is administered intravenously over 10 minutes. Roughly 45 minutes later, while emerging, she develops agitation, diaphoresis, hyperthermia to 39.4°C, tachycardia, and sustained ankle clonus with hyperreflexia. Which mechanism best explains this clinical picture?

choices.text: Methylene blue inhibited monoamine oxidase, and combined with the SSRI produced excess synaptic serotonin
choices.rationale: Correct: methylene blue is a potent monoamine oxidase inhibitor, and in a patient on an SSRI this precipitates serotonin toxicity with hyperthermia, clonus, and autonomic instability.

choices.text: Methylene blue induced methemoglobinemia, causing tissue hypoxia and agitation
choices.rationale: Incorrect: at this dose methylene blue treats rather than causes methemoglobinemia, and methemoglobinemia does not produce clonus or hyperreflexia.

choices.text: The patient developed malignant hyperthermia triggered by methylene blue
choices.rationale: Incorrect: methylene blue is not a malignant hyperthermia trigger, and MH classically presents with rising end-tidal CO2 and masseter rigidity rather than clonus and hyperreflexia.

choices.text: Methylene blue caused a direct anaphylactoid histamine release reaction
choices.rationale: Incorrect: anaphylaxis presents with hypotension, bronchospasm, and urticaria, not hyperthermia with clonus and hyperreflexia.

choices.text: Fluoxetine withdrawal precipitated a discontinuation syndrome under anesthesia
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a single perioperative dose does not cause withdrawal, and discontinuation syndrome does not produce hyperthermia with clonus.

explanation: Methylene blue is a potent inhibitor of monoamine oxidase, and when given to patients taking serotonergic agents such as SSRIs, SNRIs, or MAOIs it can precipitate serotonin syndrome—a clinical triad of altered mental status, autonomic instability, and neuromuscular hyperactivity (clonus, hyperreflexia, hyperthermia). This is a well-recognized perioperative hazard, particularly during parathyroid and other procedures where methylene blue is used for tissue localization. When feasible, alternatives to methylene blue should be sought in patients on serotonergic medications, and treatment of serotonin toxicity is supportive with cooling, benzodiazepines, and cyproheptadine.
subtopic: Perioperative drug interactions — methylene blue and serotonin syndrome

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The stem is a textbook perioperative serotonin syndrome presentation — agitation, diaphoresis, hyperthermia (39.4°C), tachycardia, and sustained clonus with hyperreflexia (Hunter criteria) after methylene blue in a patient on fluoxetine (long-half-life SSRI). The keyed answer (index 0) reflects the established and current mechanism: methylene blue is a potent MAO-A inhibitor (FDA Drug Safety Communication 2011; Ramsay/Gillman pharmacology literature), which combined with an SSRI raises synaptic serotonin. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly refuted: at 1 mg/kg methylene blue treats rather than causes methemoglobinemia and that does not cause clonus; methylene blue is not an MH trigger and MH features rising EtCO2/rigidity not clonus; anaphylaxis gives hypotension/bronchospasm not hyperthermia+clonus; and the patient is taking fluoxetine (not withdrawing), so discontinuation syndrome is doubly wrong. Dose, timing, and the treatment statement (cooling, benzodiazepines, cyproheptadine) are all accurate. REFERENCE: The reference_url (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK557593/) resolves to StatPearls 'Methylene Blue' and is genuinely on-topic — it explicitly warns of serotonin syndrome when methylene blue is co-administered with serotonergic agents (SSRIs/SNRIs/MAOIs). The page frames the mechanism as a drug interaction rather than spelling out MAO-A inhibition, but it supports the scenario and is an appropriate citation. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 58-year-old man with a T1 glottic carcinoma is undergoing CO2 laser excision of a vocal cord lesion under general anesthesia. A laser-resistant double-cuffed endotracheal tube is in place with the proximal cuff filled with methylene-blue-tinted saline, and the FiO2 has been kept at 0.30 with the balance air. While the surgeon is applying the laser near the posterior cord, the anesthetist sees a flash and flame at the surgical field and notes blue-tinted fluid in the surgical suction. What is the most appropriate FIRST action?

choices.text: Disconnect the breathing circuit and remove the endotracheal tube
choices.rationale: Correct: stopping the flow of oxidizer and removing the burning fuel source (the tube) is the priority first step in an airway fire, performed essentially simultaneously to halt combustion before it propagates down the airway.

choices.text: Increase FiO2 to 1.0 to treat the resulting hypoxemia
choices.rationale: Incorrect: oxygen is the oxidizer feeding the fire, so increasing FiO2 would intensify combustion and worsen airway injury.

choices.text: Deepen anesthesia with a propofol bolus before addressing the airway
choices.rationale: Incorrect: pharmacologic intervention delays the critical step of cutting off the oxidizer and removing the burning tube, allowing the fire to extend into the trachea.

choices.text: Deflate the proximal cuff and advance the tube distally to bypass the burn
choices.rationale: Incorrect: manipulating or advancing a burning tube spreads thermal injury deeper into the airway and does not extinguish the fire.

choices.text: Flood the surgical field with saline while continuing ventilation with the tube in place
choices.rationale: Incorrect: while saline is used to extinguish residual embers, continuing to ventilate (delivering oxidizer) through a tube that is on fire perpetuates the blaze and forces hot gases distally.

explanation: The first response to an airway fire is to simultaneously stop the flow of airway gases (disconnect the circuit) and remove the endotracheal tube, which is the burning fuel source; saline is then poured into the field to extinguish residual fire. Only after the fire is out should the patient be ventilated by mask, the airway re-secured, and the trachea examined (often by bronchoscopy) for thermal injury. Prevention centers on the lowest feasible FiO2 (<0.30) without nitrous oxide, a laser-resistant tube, and cuff saline tinted with methylene blue so a perforation—as occurred here—is immediately visible.
subtopic: Airway fire during laser laryngeal surgery — immediate management

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer (disconnect circuit + remove ETT) is the single best first action and matches the ASA Practice Advisory/APSF airway-fire protocol: stop airway gas flow and remove the burning tube simultaneously, THEN saline to extinguish, then mask-ventilate, re-secure airway, and bronchoscopy for thermal injury. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and the rationales are accurate: raising FiO2 to 1.0 adds oxidizer (harmful); a propofol bolus is a dangerous delay; advancing the burning tube spreads thermal injury distally; flooding the field while still ventilating through a burning tube perpetuates the fire and drives hot gas down the airway (saline is correct only after tube removal). The scenario is internally consistent — blue-tinted fluid in suction signals a cuff perforation exposing the airway near the laser, which is exactly why methylene-blue cuff tinting is recommended. Minor, non-disqualifying nuance: explanation says 'lowest feasible FiO2 (<0.30)' while the stem ran 0.30 (the boundary of the standard 'begin <30%' guidance) — not a contradiction and does not affect the key. REFERENCE: https://www.openanesthesia.org/airway_fire_mgmt/ resolves to 'Surgical Fires — OpenAnesthesia' (updated Feb 2023), which directly covers airway fire prevention/management, the fire triangle, the <30% O2 starting concentration, laser-surgery fire risk, and ordered airway-fire response — title and topic both match. Both clinical and reference dimensions are sound.

stem: A 44-year-old man presents to the OR for incision and drainage of a rapidly progressive bilateral submandibular and sublingual cellulitis arising from an infected mandibular molar (Ludwig angina). He has a muffled "hot-potato" voice, drooling, tender brawny induration of the floor of the mouth with tongue elevation, and trismus limiting mouth opening to about 1.5 cm. He is sitting upright, anxious, with SpO2 95% on a non-rebreather. Which airway approach is most appropriate to secure the airway?

choices.text: Inhalational induction with sevoflurane, then direct laryngoscopy once deeply anesthetized
choices.rationale: Incorrect: loss of muscle tone and reflexes with general anesthesia can precipitate complete airway obstruction in a patient whose patency depends on upright positioning and spontaneous effort, and trismus plus tongue elevation make laryngoscopy and rescue ventilation unreliable.

choices.text: Rapid sequence induction with propofol and rocuronium followed by video laryngoscopy
choices.rationale: Incorrect: paralysis abolishes spontaneous ventilation in a patient who may be impossible to mask-ventilate or intubate due to floor-of-mouth distortion and trismus, risking a 'can't intubate, can't oxygenate' catastrophe.

choices.text: Awake flexible fiberoptic nasotracheal intubation with topicalization, with a surgeon and equipment for emergent surgical airway immediately available
choices.rationale: Correct: maintaining spontaneous ventilation and protective reflexes via awake fiberoptic intubation is the preferred technique, with double set-up for cricothyrotomy/tracheostomy because deterioration to a surgical airway must be anticipated.

choices.text: Awake blind nasotracheal intubation advanced during spontaneous breathing
choices.rationale: Incorrect: blind passage can rupture an abscess, provoke bleeding, worsen edema, and trigger laryngospasm, all of which can convert a difficult airway into an unmanageable one.

choices.text: Placement of a supraglottic airway (LMA) under deep sedation to ventilate during drainage
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a supraglottic device does not bypass or protect against the supraglottic and floor-of-mouth swelling that characterizes Ludwig angina and provides no definitive control of a deteriorating airway.

explanation: Ludwig angina produces rapidly progressive submandibular/sublingual cellulitis with posterior tongue displacement, trismus, and distorted anatomy that makes both mask ventilation and laryngoscopy unreliable. Awake flexible fiberoptic (commonly nasotracheal) intubation under topical anesthesia preserves spontaneous ventilation and airway reflexes and is the preferred technique, with a surgeon prepared for awake tracheostomy or cricothyrotomy as the immediate backup. Inducing general anesthesia or giving a paralytic risks total obstruction with no reliable rescue, and blind techniques risk abscess rupture and bleeding.
subtopic: Deep neck space infection (Ludwig angina) — anticipated difficult airway

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (C, index 2) — awake flexible fiberoptic nasotracheal intubation with topicalization plus immediate surgeon/equipment for an emergent surgical airway (double set-up) — is the single best answer for Ludwig angina with an anticipated difficult airway. Maintaining spontaneous ventilation and protective reflexes is the accepted standard. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly explained: inhalational induction with DL (A) and RSI with paralysis (B) both risk loss of tone/CICO in a patient whose patency depends on upright positioning and spontaneous effort, with trismus (1.5 cm) and floor-of-mouth distortion making mask ventilation and laryngoscopy unreliable; blind nasotracheal (D) risks abscess rupture, bleeding, edema, and laryngospasm; LMA under deep sedation (E) does not bypass supraglottic/floor-of-mouth swelling and provides no definitive control. The stem is internally consistent and unambiguous, and the explanation is accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482354/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Ludwig Angina' article, which directly supports the keyed approach (awake fiberoptic nasotracheal intubation as gold standard, surgical airway prepared beforehand) and even matches the blind-nasotracheal distractor rationale nearly verbatim. Citation is live and on-topic.

stem: A 5-year-old girl returns to the OR 7 hours after an outpatient tonsillectomy with brisk oropharyngeal bleeding. She has been swallowing blood and vomited a large amount of dark clot in the holding area. She is pale and tachycardic at 140/min with a blood pressure of 88/52 mmHg; a 22-gauge IV is in place and a fluid bolus is running, with blood ordered. After ensuring suction, a second IV, and resuscitation, which induction strategy best addresses the principal anesthetic hazards of this patient?

choices.text: Volume resuscitation and then a rapid sequence induction with suction immediately available, anticipating a difficult, blood-obscured laryngoscopy and hypovolemia
choices.rationale: Correct: the child has a full stomach of swallowed blood (aspiration risk) plus hypovolemia, so resuscitation followed by RSII with two functioning suctions and a plan for poor laryngoscopic view directly targets both principal hazards.

choices.text: Inhalational induction with spontaneous ventilation, accepting the full stomach to preserve the airway
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a slow inhalational induction in a child with active bleeding and a stomach full of blood markedly increases aspiration risk and is poorly tolerated in hypovolemia.

choices.text: Proceed with standard induction without additional IV access or volume, since the bleeding is in the surgeon's field
choices.rationale: Incorrect: this child shows signs of significant hypovolemia and ongoing blood loss, so inducing without resuscitation and adequate access can precipitate cardiovascular collapse.

choices.text: Awake nasal fiberoptic intubation without sedation to avoid loss of airway reflexes
choices.rationale: Incorrect: awake fiberoptic intubation in an uncooperative bleeding child is impractical, and blood in the airway obscures the fiberoptic view, making this approach unreliable here.

choices.text: Defer surgery and observe, since most post-tonsillectomy bleeds stop spontaneously
choices.rationale: Incorrect: active secondary hemorrhage with hemodynamic instability requires prompt surgical control and cannot be safely managed by observation alone.

explanation: Post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage presents two simultaneous anesthetic problems: a full stomach of swallowed blood (high aspiration risk) and hypovolemia from blood loss that is often underestimated because much is swallowed. Management begins with resuscitation (volume, adequate IV access, blood available) followed by a rapid sequence induction with two suctions ready, since laryngoscopy is frequently obscured by ongoing bleeding and clot. Hypoxemia during induction is actually more common than aspiration in this population, so being prepared for a difficult, blood-filled view and having backup airway equipment is essential.
subtopic: Post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage — full stomach and hypovolemia
reference_title: Pediatric Anesthesia Management for Post-Tonsillectomy Bleed: Current Status and Future Directions

verdict.reason:
CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The stem is a textbook secondary post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage (7h post-op, brisk bleeding, swallowed blood + vomited clot, tachycardia 140, BP 88/52 indicating hypovolemia). The keyed answer (volume resuscitation then RSII with suction available, anticipating blood-obscured laryngoscopy and hypovolemia) is the established standard of care and is the single best answer — it uniquely addresses BOTH principal hazards (full stomach/aspiration and hypovolemia). All four distractors are genuinely inferior: inhalational spontaneous-ventilation induction increases aspiration risk and is poorly tolerated in hypovolemia; standard induction without resuscitation/access risks cardiovascular collapse; awake nasal fiberoptic in an uncooperative bleeding 5-year-old is impractical and the scope view is obscured by blood; observation is unsafe with active hemorrhage and hemodynamic instability. The explanation's claim that hypoxemia is actually more common than aspiration in this population is correct and directly supported by the cited literature. Minor (non-disqualifying) note: optimal practice also includes reduced/hemodynamically-neutral induction agent dosing in hypovolemia, but the keyed answer makes no incorrect drug claim, so this does not undermine it.

REFERENCE: Verified via WebFetch. URL https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8749229/ resolves; title matches exactly ("Pediatric Anesthesia Management for Post-Tonsillectomy Bleed: Current Status and Future Directions," Int J Gen Med, 2022). The page is squarely on-topic: it discusses RSII (classical vs controlled), aspiration vs hypoxemia risk, fluid resuscitation/IV access, multiple ETT sizes, and dual suction — directly supporting the stem, key, and explanation (including the hypoxemia-vs-aspiration point). No reference problem.

stem: A 58-year-old woman presents for urgent surgical decompression of a pathologic vertebral fracture. She has primary hyperparathyroidism from a parathyroid adenoma that has not yet been resected. She is lethargic and confused, with a heart rate of 58/min and blood pressure 148/86 mmHg. Labs show a serum calcium of 14.8 mg/dL (corrected), phosphate 2.1 mg/dL, and a normal potassium. The surgical team wants to proceed within hours. Which intervention should be initiated FIRST to address her calcium derangement before induction?

choices.text: Aggressive isotonic saline volume expansion
choices.rationale: Correct: hypercalcemic patients are profoundly volume-depleted from calcium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, and rehydration with isotonic saline both restores intravascular volume and promotes calciuresis, making it the essential first step.

choices.text: Intravenous loop diuretic (furosemide) as the primary therapy
choices.rationale: Incorrect: loop diuretics are no longer recommended as first-line and worsen hypovolemia and electrolyte loss if given before the patient is rehydrated.

choices.text: Intravenous bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid) as the sole acute measure
choices.rationale: Incorrect: bisphosphonates are valuable but take 24-72 hours to lower calcium and do not address the acute volume deficit or provide rapid correction before surgery.

choices.text: Subcutaneous calcitonin as definitive monotherapy
choices.rationale: Incorrect: calcitonin lowers calcium modestly and transiently and develops tachyphylaxis within 48 hours, so it is an adjunct rather than the foundational first intervention.

choices.text: Emergent hemodialysis against a low-calcium bath
choices.rationale: Incorrect: dialysis is reserved for renal failure or life-threatening hypercalcemia refractory to volume resuscitation, not as the initial maneuver in a patient who can be hydrated.

explanation: Severe hypercalcemia causes osmotic diuresis and vomiting that leave patients markedly volume-depleted, so isotonic saline rehydration is the first and most important step, both restoring perfusion and enhancing renal calcium excretion. Calcitonin can be added for faster onset and bisphosphonates for durable control, but these are adjuncts. Furosemide is given only after euvolemia is restored, and dialysis is reserved for refractory or renally impaired cases. Intraoperatively, hypercalcemia can shorten the QT interval, blunt the response to nondepolarizing relaxants, and predispose to dysrhythmias.
subtopic: Primary hyperparathyroidism - perioperative hypercalcemic crisis management

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (aggressive isotonic saline volume expansion) is the single best, unambiguous FIRST intervention for severe hypercalcemia — universally endorsed across endocrine and anesthesia sources. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and their rationales are accurate and current: loop diuretics are no longer first-line and worsen hypovolemia if given before rehydration; bisphosphonates take 24-72h and don't address volume; calcitonin is modest/transient with tachyphylaxis; dialysis is reserved for renal failure/refractory cases. The intraoperative pearls (shortened QT, dysrhythmia risk, relative resistance to nondepolarizing relaxants) are correct. The only minor imprecision is the rationale attributing volume depletion to "calcium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus" while the explanation cites osmotic diuresis/vomiting — both are legitimately described mechanisms (hypercalcemia does cause nephrogenic DI via the calcium-sensing receptor), so this is not an error and does not affect the answer. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430714/ resolves to the StatPearls "Hypercalcemia" chapter, which explicitly covers saline hydration as the volume-restoring measure, calcitonin's rapid-but-transient effect, the ~3-day bisphosphonate onset, and dialysis reserved for renal failure. Citation is valid and directly on-topic. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 44-year-old man with episodes of diaphoresis, palpitations, and confusion relieved by eating is scheduled for laparoscopic enucleation of a pancreatic insulinoma. His preoperative fasting glucose was 41 mg/dL with inappropriately elevated insulin and C-peptide. An arterial line and frequent point-of-care glucose monitoring are in place. As the surgeon manipulates and then ligates the vessels supplying the tumor, which glucose pattern should the anesthetist most anticipate, and what is the appropriate response?

choices.text: A transient drop in glucose during tumor handling, then a rise after devascularization; treat by reducing or stopping supplemental dextrose
choices.rationale: Correct: manipulation can provoke insulin release and worsen hypoglycemia, but once the tumor's blood supply is ligated, insulin secretion falls and glucose rebounds, so dextrose infusions should be down-titrated to avoid rebound hyperglycemia.

choices.text: A sustained fall in glucose that persists unchanged after the tumor is removed, requiring a continued high-dose dextrose infusion
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the hallmark of successful resection is that hypoglycemia resolves after devascularization, so persistent unchanged hypoglycemia would suggest residual or multifocal tumor, not the expected pattern.

choices.text: Immediate severe hyperglycemia at the moment of skin incision before any tumor manipulation
choices.rationale: Incorrect: glucose changes are driven by tumor handling and devascularization, not by incision, so hyperglycemia at incision is not the anticipated dynamic.

choices.text: No meaningful glucose change throughout the case because tight insulin autoregulation buffers manipulation
choices.rationale: Incorrect: insulinomas secrete insulin autonomously without normal feedback, so manipulation and removal produce clinically important glucose swings.

choices.text: A glucose rise during manipulation followed by profound hypoglycemia after devascularization
choices.rationale: Incorrect: this reverses the actual physiology, since manipulation tends to release insulin and lower glucose while devascularization removes the insulin source and raises it.

explanation: Insulinomas secrete insulin autonomously, so surgical manipulation can transiently increase insulin release and deepen hypoglycemia, while ligation of the tumor's blood supply abruptly removes the insulin source and produces a rebound rise in glucose. An intraoperative glucose increase of roughly 30 mg/dL or more after resection is often used as evidence of complete removal. The anesthetist should monitor glucose frequently, maintain a dextrose infusion before excision, and then down-titrate it afterward to avoid rebound hyperglycemia.
subtopic: Insulinoma resection - intraoperative glucose dynamics around tumor devascularization

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer (index 0) is the single best answer and reflects standard anesthesia teaching: insulinomas secrete insulin autonomously without feedback; surgical manipulation can transiently deepen hypoglycemia, and ligation/devascularization abruptly removes the insulin source producing rebound hyperglycemia, so dextrose should be down-titrated after excision to avoid rebound hyperglycemia. The ~30 mg/dL post-resection glucose rise as a marker of complete excision is a recognized intraoperative landmark. All distractors are genuinely inferior: persistent unchanged hypoglycemia (index 1) implies residual/multifocal tumor; hyperglycemia at incision (index 2) is not the driver; no-change-due-to-autoregulation (index 3) is wrong because insulinomas lack feedback; index 4 reverses the physiology (good near-miss). Rationales are accurate and current. Stem is unambiguous. Minor real-world nuance (manipulation-induced dip is variable) does not undermine option 0 as the best choice. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK544299/ resolves to StatPearls 'Insulinoma,' which is genuinely on-topic and covers surgical resection/enucleation; an accurate, relevant citation for the subject even though it does not elaborate the specific intraoperative glucose-rebound detail.

stem: A 7-year-old, 24-kg child is under general endotracheal anesthesia with sevoflurane for bilateral medial rectus recession to correct esotropia. As the surgeon places traction on the right medial rectus muscle, the monitor shows the heart rate abruptly fall from 96 to 38 beats per minute with a stable blood pressure of 88/52 mmHg. No anticholinergic was given before incision. What is the most appropriate immediate action?

choices.text: Ask the surgeon to release traction on the extraocular muscle
choices.rationale: Correct: the oculocardiac reflex (afferent trigeminal V1, efferent vagus) is fatigable, and ceasing the mechanical stimulus is the first-line intervention and usually terminates the bradycardia within seconds.

choices.text: Administer atropine 10-20 mcg/kg IV immediately before any other step
choices.rationale: Incorrect: anticholinergics are reserved for bradycardia that persists or recurs after the stimulus is removed, or that is hemodynamically significant, not as the reflexive first move.

choices.text: Begin chest compressions per pediatric ACLS
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the patient has a perfusing rhythm with a stable blood pressure, so compressions are not indicated for a transient reflex bradycardia.

choices.text: Deepen anesthesia with a propofol bolus to blunt the reflex
choices.rationale: Incorrect: deepening anesthesia does not reliably abort an active reflex and a propofol bolus risks worsening hypotension during the bradycardic episode.

choices.text: Give glycopyrrolate 10 mcg/kg IV because of its longer duration
choices.rationale: Incorrect: pharmacologic treatment is not the immediate step, and glycopyrrolate's slower onset (1-2 min) makes it less ideal than atropine even when an anticholinergic is eventually warranted.

explanation: The oculocardiac reflex is triggered by traction on the extraocular muscles or pressure on the globe and is most common in pediatric strabismus surgery. Because the reflex fatigues with repeated stimulation, the definitive first step is to have the surgeon stop the inciting stimulus, which typically restores the heart rate within seconds. Anticholinergics (atropine 10-20 mcg/kg IV or glycopyrrolate 10 mcg/kg IV) are added only if bradycardia persists, recurs, or causes hemodynamic compromise.
subtopic: Oculocardiac reflex management during strabismus surgery

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (release traction on the extraocular muscle) is the single best, unambiguous answer for an active oculocardiac reflex (OCR). The stem is a classic OCR presentation: pediatric strabismus surgery, abrupt bradycardia (96->38) on medial rectus traction, hemodynamically tolerated (stable BP 88/52), no anticholinergic given. Because OCR is fatigable, removing the inciting stimulus is first-line and typically restores HR within seconds. All distractors are genuinely inferior: atropine "before any other step" is wrong because anticholinergics are reserved for persistent/recurrent/hemodynamically significant bradycardia (BP is stable here); chest compressions are wrong with a perfusing rhythm; a propofol bolus does not reliably abort the reflex and risks hypotension; giving glycopyrrolate is not the immediate step and its IV onset (~1-2 min) is slower than atropine. The pathway description (afferent trigeminal V/V1, efferent vagus = trigeminovagal reflex) and anticholinergic dosing (atropine 10-20 mcg/kg IV, glycopyrrolate 10 mcg/kg IV) are accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/oculocardiac-reflex/ resolves (active page, last updated 01/20/2023) and is directly on-topic; its content (definition, V afferent/X efferent pathway, strabismus trigger, "cease the stimulus first" management, and the identical atropine/glycopyrrolate doses) corroborates the question. Both clinical content and citation are sound.

stem: A 58-year-old man with diet-controlled type 2 diabetes is scheduled for cataract extraction under general anesthesia. He underwent pars plana vitrectomy with sulfur hexafluoride (SF6) gas tamponade in the same eye 10 days ago for a macular hole; he was instructed to maintain face-down positioning. The retina surgeon has cleared him, and the anesthesiologist is selecting an anesthetic technique. Which intraoperative decision is most important to prevent catastrophic visual loss?

choices.text: Avoid nitrous oxide entirely during the anesthetic
choices.rationale: Correct: nitrous oxide diffuses into the closed intraocular gas bubble faster than the gas leaves, expanding the bubble and raising intraocular pressure enough to occlude the central retinal artery.

choices.text: Avoid succinylcholine and use rocuronium for muscle relaxation
choices.rationale: Incorrect: although succinylcholine transiently raises intraocular pressure, the dominant and catastrophic hazard with a persistent gas bubble is nitrous oxide-driven bubble expansion, not the relaxant choice.

choices.text: Limit peak inspired oxygen to 30% to avoid bubble expansion
choices.rationale: Incorrect: oxygen does not expand the bubble the way nitrous oxide does, and restricting FiO2 offers no protection against gas expansion.

choices.text: Keep the patient strictly normocapnic to prevent intraocular pressure spikes
choices.rationale: Incorrect: modest CO2-mediated changes in intraocular pressure are trivial compared with the dramatic pressure rise from nitrous oxide diffusing into the gas bubble.

choices.text: Maintain head-up positioning throughout the case to lower intraocular pressure
choices.rationale: Incorrect: positioning does not counteract the volume expansion of an intraocular gas bubble caused by nitrous oxide and would not prevent retinal artery occlusion.

explanation: An intraocular gas bubble (SF6, C2F6, or C3F8) creates a closed gas space; nitrous oxide is far more soluble than these gases and diffuses in faster than they diffuse out, expanding the bubble and acutely raising intraocular pressure to the point of central retinal artery occlusion and blindness. Patients remain at risk as long as the bubble persists, which for SF6 is roughly several weeks; nitrous oxide should be avoided for the duration. The safe practice is to omit nitrous oxide entirely (and ideally flag the patient with a warning bracelet) until the gas has fully reabsorbed.
subtopic: Intraocular gas tamponade and nitrous oxide interaction
reference_title: Nitrous oxide anaesthesia and intraocular gases (British Journal of Anaesthesia)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (avoid nitrous oxide entirely) is unambiguously the single best answer. N2O is far more soluble than the inert tamponade gases (SF6/C2F6/C3F8), diffuses into the closed intraocular bubble faster than the gas leaves, expands it, and acutely raises IOP enough to occlude the central retinal artery and cause blindness — the dominant catastrophic hazard. At 10 days post-vitrectomy an SF6 bubble can still be present (SF6 reabsorbs over ~1-2+ weeks), so the precaution applies. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: succinylcholine's transient IOP rise, FiO2 (O2 does not expand the bubble), normocapnia (trivial CO2-mediated IOP change), and head-up positioning (does not counteract volume expansion) are all correctly characterized. The surgeon "clearing" the patient is an intentional trap testing that the anesthesiologist must independently omit N2O — good design, not ambiguity. Minor nit: the explanation's "several weeks" for SF6 reabsorption is slightly generous (SF6 is shorter-acting than C3F8) but errs toward caution and is clinically safe/defensible. REFERENCE: WebFetch resolved successfully. The URL points to the genuine BJA article 'Nitrous oxide anaesthesia and intraocular gases' (A.S.L. Kwan, Br J Anaesth 2005;95(5):719), which directly documents N2O-driven IOP rise (~100% within ~24 min), retinal artery occlusion, and visual loss with intraocular gas — exactly on-topic. Title and citation match. No reference problem.

stem: A 22-year-old man is brought to the operating room for repair of a penetrating open globe injury sustained 90 minutes ago. He ate a large meal shortly before the injury, has no other trauma, and his airway exam is reassuring. He is in pain and anxious, with a heart rate of 104 and blood pressure 138/82 mmHg. The surgeon wants to proceed urgently to preserve the eye. Which induction approach best balances the competing risks in this patient?

choices.text: Inhalational induction with sevoflurane preserving spontaneous ventilation, then intubate deep without relaxant
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a slow inhalational induction in a patient with a full stomach markedly increases aspiration risk and provides poor protection against coughing and IOP spikes.

choices.text: Awake fiberoptic intubation under topical and nerve-block local anesthesia
choices.rationale: Incorrect: an awake intubation provokes coughing, bucking, and a Valsalva response that sharply raise intraocular pressure and risk extrusion of ocular contents in an open globe.

choices.text: Rapid sequence induction with adequate propofol followed by succinylcholine, with cricoid pressure
choices.rationale: Correct: a properly dosed induction agent blunts the small succinylcholine-related IOP rise, and there are no reports of content extrusion when succinylcholine is given with adequate anesthesia, while rapid sequence protects the full-stomach airway.

choices.text: Deferring surgery until the stomach empties to eliminate aspiration risk
choices.rationale: Incorrect: open globe repair is time-sensitive for visual outcome and infection prevention, and gastric emptying is unreliable after trauma and opioid analgesia.

choices.text: Rapid sequence induction using a low 0.6 mg/kg dose of rocuronium to speed onset
choices.rationale: Incorrect: 0.6 mg/kg rocuronium gives a slower, less reliable onset than a full RSI dose, prolonging the window of aspiration risk; if a nondepolarizer is chosen, 1.2 mg/kg is the appropriate RSI dose.

explanation: Open globe injury with a full stomach pits aspiration risk against the risk of raising intraocular pressure and extruding ocular contents. Rapid sequence induction is the preferred technique, and although succinylcholine transiently raises IOP by 10-20 mmHg, no published cases describe globe content extrusion when it is given together with an adequate induction dose, so it remains acceptable and provides excellent, fast intubating conditions. If a nondepolarizing relaxant is preferred instead, a full RSI dose of rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg (not 0.6 mg/kg) is required to match the rapid onset needed to secure the airway.
subtopic: Open globe injury with full stomach: induction and muscle relaxant choice

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The keyed answer (RSI with adequate propofol + succinylcholine + cricoid pressure) is the single best of the five offered options for an open globe with a full stomach. Succinylcholine transiently raises IOP ~10-20 mmHg, but the standard board/consensus teaching (and the cited source) is that no globe-content extrusion has been reported when it is given with an adequate induction dose, and RSI protects the aspiration-prone airway. The potential competitor — a high-dose rocuronium RSI (1.2 mg/kg) that avoids the IOP rise — is deliberately NOT offered; the only rocuronium choice (E) uses the wrong RSI dose (0.6 mg/kg), which is genuinely inferior (slower, less reliable onset). All distractors are correctly inferior: inhalational spontaneous-vent induction (A) is aspiration-risky with a full stomach; awake intubation (B) provokes coughing/Valsalva and IOP spikes (and blocks pressurize an open globe); deferring surgery (D) ignores the time-sensitivity of open globe repair and unreliable gastric emptying after trauma/opioids. Magnitudes, dosing, and rationales are accurate and current. REFERENCE: https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/ocular-trauma/ resolves (no 404) and is squarely on-topic. It explicitly states RSI is preferred for open globe, that succinylcholine has 'no published reports of extrusion of ocular contents when administered along with an adequate dose of an anesthetic induction agent,' and that 1.2 mg/kg rocuronium is the nondepolarizing RSI alternative — matching the explanation verbatim. Both clinical and reference dimensions are sound; keep.

stem: A healthy 4-year-old boy is undergoing bilateral myringotomy and tube placement under sevoflurane via face mask without an IV. Shortly after the surgeon manipulates the ear and the depth of anesthesia is light, the child develops complete airway obstruction: there is no chest rise, the reservoir bag does not move with attempted positive-pressure ventilation, paradoxical chest-abdominal motion is present, and SpO2 falls from 99% to 84%. Gentle continuous positive airway pressure with 100% oxygen and a jaw thrust does not relieve the obstruction. There is still no intravenous access. Which intervention is the most appropriate immediate next step?

choices.text: Insert a laryngeal mask airway to bypass the obstruction
choices.rationale: A supraglottic device cannot open vocal cords that are spasmodically adducted at the glottis and forcing instrumentation during laryngospasm worsens the reflex, so it is not the immediate maneuver.

choices.text: Apply firm bilateral pressure at the laryngospasm notch (Larson maneuver) while continuing CPAP, and give intramuscular succinylcholine if spasm persists
choices.rationale: Bilateral firm pressure behind the posterior ramus of the mandible (the laryngospasm notch) combined with CPAP often breaks laryngospasm, and IM succinylcholine is the definitive treatment when there is no IV access and spasm is refractory.

choices.text: Deliver several forceful high-pressure positive-pressure breaths to overcome the closed cords
choices.rationale: High airway pressures against closed cords insufflate the stomach, raising aspiration risk and worsening ventilation, and they do not relieve true glottic closure.

choices.text: Stop all oxygen and allow the child to wake up spontaneously
choices.rationale: Withdrawing oxygen during desaturating laryngospasm risks profound hypoxia, bradycardia, and arrest and is never appropriate management.

choices.text: Perform an immediate surgical cricothyrotomy
choices.rationale: Cricothyrotomy is a last resort for a true can't-intubate-can't-oxygenate scenario after pharmacologic and maneuver-based measures fail, not the first step in reversible laryngospasm.

explanation: Laryngospasm is a sustained glottic closure reflex most common in young children at light planes of anesthesia and during airway stimulation such as ear manipulation. First-line management is removing the stimulus, applying CPAP with 100% oxygen and a vigorous jaw thrust, and applying firm bilateral pressure at the laryngospasm notch (behind the posterior ramus of the mandible at the styloid). When spasm is refractory and there is no IV, intramuscular succinylcholine (with atropine to blunt bradycardia in small children) is the definitive treatment to relax the cords before hypoxic bradyarrest develops.

verdict.reason: Clinical content is correct and current.
