Citation matches the content.

stem: A 5-year-old girl returns to the emergency department 7 hours after tonsillectomy with active oral bleeding. Her parents report she has been swallowing repeatedly and vomited a moderate amount of dark blood en route. She is pale and tachycardic with a heart rate of 148, blood pressure 92/58, capillary refill 3 seconds, and she last ate solids this morning. The ENT surgeon requests general anesthesia for surgical control of the bleeding tonsillar bed. Which anesthetic plan is most appropriate?

choices.text: Inhalational induction with sevoflurane maintaining spontaneous ventilation, then intubate without neuromuscular blockade
choices.rationale: A slow inhalational induction in a child with a stomach full of swallowed blood markedly increases aspiration risk and is poorly tolerated in a hypovolemic patient.

choices.text: Awake fiberoptic intubation under topical anesthesia
choices.rationale: Awake fiberoptic technique is impractical and dangerous in a frightened, actively bleeding young child whose blood obscures the view and who cannot cooperate.

choices.text: Establish/confirm IV access, begin volume resuscitation, ensure two working suction setups, and perform a rapid sequence induction with a reduced-dose induction agent plus a rapid-onset neuromuscular blocker
choices.rationale: These patients are full-stomach and often hypovolemic and anemic, so resuscitation, redundant suction, and an RSI with a reduced induction-agent dose and a rapid-onset relaxant best protect the airway while limiting hemodynamic collapse.

choices.text: Proceed immediately with a full induction dose of propofol and mask ventilation before muscle relaxant
choices.rationale: A full propofol dose in a hypovolemic child can precipitate profound hypotension, and prolonged mask ventilation before securing the airway does not address the aspiration risk.

choices.text: Cancel and reschedule the case until the child has been NPO for 8 hours
choices.rationale: Post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage is a surgical emergency that cannot wait, and the stomach is full of blood regardless of NPO time so delaying does not reduce aspiration risk.

explanation: A child with post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage is assumed to have a stomach full of swallowed blood (full-stomach, aspiration risk) and may be significantly hypovolemic and anemic, with deceptively preserved vitals until roughly 40% of blood volume is lost. The plan is to secure IV access, resuscitate with fluid/blood, prepare two suction setups and a smaller endotracheal tube, and perform a rapid sequence induction with a reduced dose of induction agent and a rapid-onset neuromuscular blocker. After intubation the stomach should be decompressed with a large-bore orogastric tube.
subtopic: Pediatric ENT/shared-airway emergencies — post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage

verdict.reason: Clinical: The keyed answer (index 2) is the unambiguous single best answer for pediatric post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage — confirm/establish IV access, volume resuscitate, ensure two suction setups, and perform RSI with a reduced-dose induction agent plus a rapid-onset neuromuscular blocker. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and their rationales are accurate: inhalational induction with spontaneous ventilation provides inadequate aspiration protection in a full-stomach child and is poorly tolerated in hypovolemia (index 0); awake fiberoptic is impractical/dangerous in a frightened, uncooperative young child with a blood-obscured view (index 1); full-dose propofol risks profound hypotension and mask ventilation before securing the airway does not address aspiration (index 3); and cancelling for NPO is wrong because PTH is a surgical emergency and the stomach is full of swallowed blood regardless of NPO time (index 4). The explanation is accurate and current: full-stomach assumption, late decompensation in compensated pediatric shock (~40% blood-volume loss is a standard teaching figure), smaller ETT, two suctions, and post-intubation orogastric decompression. Reference: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia 'Posttonsillectomy Hemorrhage' page resolved successfully (last updated Feb 2023) and is directly on-topic, corroborating the keyed plan nearly point-for-point (RSI with reduced induction dose + succinylcholine/rocuronium, 10-20 mL/kg crystalloid resuscitation, two functioning suctions, varied/smaller ETT sizes, ENT surgeon present, orogastric decompression). Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 9-year-old boy with newly diagnosed mediastinal lymphoma is scheduled for an excisional lymph node biopsy to establish a tissue diagnosis. He reports a cough and dyspnea that are worse when lying flat, and he prefers to sleep sitting upright. CT shows a large anterior mediastinal mass compressing the distal trachea with roughly 60% luminal narrowing and abutting the right main pulmonary artery. The oncology team asks whether they can proceed under general anesthesia with endotracheal intubation and muscle relaxation. Which anesthetic approach is most appropriate?

choices.text: Maintain spontaneous ventilation throughout, avoid neuromuscular blockade, identify a rescue position in advance, and have rigid bronchoscopy available
choices.rationale: Preserving spontaneous ventilation and natural airway tone, avoiding paralysis, pre-identifying a rescue position, and having rigid bronchoscopy and a surgeon ready are the cornerstones that prevent loss of a critically compressed airway under anesthesia.

choices.text: Perform a rapid sequence induction with a full induction dose and rocuronium to quickly secure the airway
choices.rationale: Abolishing spontaneous ventilation and chest-wall tone with paralysis can cause the mass to collapse the trachea and great vessels, producing catastrophic obstruction or cardiovascular collapse that cannot be relieved by an endotracheal tube above the obstruction.

choices.text: Induce general anesthesia and place the patient supine for optimal surgical exposure before biopsy
choices.rationale: The supine position increases mass compression of the airway and vessels and worsens symptoms that are already positional, so it should be avoided during induction in a high-risk mass.

choices.text: Proceed with deep general anesthesia and positive-pressure ventilation, since an endotracheal tube guarantees a patent airway
choices.rationale: An endotracheal tube cannot stent open compression that occurs distal to its tip, so positive-pressure ventilation under deep anesthesia does not protect against collapse of the distal trachea or bronchi.

choices.text: Cancel surgery and start empiric high-dose radiation to shrink the mass before any anesthetic, with no further airway planning
choices.rationale: Empiric pre-biopsy radiation can destroy diagnostic tissue and is not a substitute for a safe anesthetic plan when a tissue diagnosis is still required; many such biopsies can be done safely under local or sedation with spontaneous ventilation.

explanation: A large anterior mediastinal mass can compress the trachea, mainstem bronchi, and great vessels, and these compressive effects are magnified supine and under general anesthesia when smooth muscle and chest-wall tone relax. The safest plan maintains spontaneous ventilation and natural airway tone, avoids neuromuscular blockade, pre-identifies the patient's rescue position (often lateral or prone), and has rigid bronchoscopy with an ENT/thoracic surgeon on standby. Whenever feasible, the biopsy should be obtained under local anesthesia or light sedation to avoid abolishing spontaneous ventilation.
subtopic: Pediatric thoracic anesthesia — anterior mediastinal mass
reference_title: Anterior Mediastinal Masses in Children — OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. This is a textbook high-risk anterior mediastinal mass (positional orthopnea, 60% tracheal compression >50%, PA abutment). The keyed answer (maintain spontaneous ventilation, avoid neuromuscular blockade, pre-identify rescue position, have rigid bronchoscopy/surgeon available) is the established management bundle and is clearly the single best choice. All four distractors are genuinely inferior with accurate rationales: RSI+rocuronium abolishes chest-wall tone/spontaneous ventilation and risks catastrophic distal airway/great-vessel collapse; supine positioning worsens compression in a positional patient; an ETT cannot stent compression distal to its tip so deep GA+PPV does not protect the airway; empiric pre-biopsy radiation destroys diagnostic tissue and abandons airway planning. Explanation is accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia URL resolves successfully (page last updated 02/05/2024) and is directly on-topic — it covers pediatric anterior mediastinal mass anesthetic management including maintaining spontaneous ventilation, rescue positioning (lateral/prone), and rigid bronchoscopy/ENT standby, matching the question's teaching points exactly.

stem: A 9-year-old boy with spina bifida and a neurogenic bladder requiring chronic intermittent catheterization is scheduled for a ventriculoperitoneal shunt revision. He has had multiple prior operations. During induction of general anesthesia, shortly after the surgeon dons gloves and begins prepping, he develops diffuse flushing, urticaria, wheezing, a fall in blood pressure to 62/30 mmHg, and a rising peak inspiratory pressure. Which of the following is the single most important measure that should have been in place to PREVENT this reaction in this patient?

choices.text: Administering a preoperative dose of diphenhydramine and methylprednisolone the night before surgery
choices.rationale: Pretreatment with antihistamines and corticosteroids does not reliably prevent IgE-mediated anaphylaxis and is not an evidence-based substitute for allergen avoidance.

choices.text: Avoiding all aminosteroid neuromuscular blocking agents and using cisatracurium instead
choices.rationale: Choice of neuromuscular blocker addresses a different anaphylaxis trigger and would not prevent a reaction driven by latex exposure from gloves and supplies.

choices.text: Scheduling the case as the last procedure of the day to minimize aerosolized particles
choices.rationale: Latex-sensitive patients should be scheduled as the FIRST case of the day to minimize accumulated airborne latex glove powder, so scheduling last is incorrect.

choices.text: Establishing a latex-free environment using only non-latex gloves and equipment throughout the perioperative period
choices.rationale: Creating a strictly latex-free environment is the cornerstone of preventing reactions in a high-risk spina bifida patient, whose 20-65% latex sensitization rate makes avoidance essential.

choices.text: Obtaining a serum tryptase level on the morning of surgery before induction
choices.rationale: Tryptase confirms mast cell degranulation after a reaction occurs but is a diagnostic, not preventive, measure.

explanation: Children with spina bifida have latex sensitization rates of roughly 20-65% owing to repeated surgeries and bladder catheterizations, making them the prototypical high-risk group for perioperative latex anaphylaxis. The single most effective preventive strategy is strict avoidance: a latex-free environment with non-latex gloves, catheters, tourniquets, and supplies. Latex-sensitive patients should also be scheduled as the first case of the day, and pharmacologic premedication does not reliably prevent IgE-mediated anaphylaxis.
subtopic: Latex allergy and perioperative allergic reactions

The keyed answer D (strict latex-free environment) is the single best PREVENTIVE measure and is unambiguous. The explanation is accurate and current (sensitization 20-65%, first-case scheduling, latex-free supplies).\n\nREFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK545164/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Latex Allergy' article (Nguyen & Kohli, updated 2023). It is directly on-topic: it discusses spina bifida as a high-risk group, states sensitization prevalence of 20-65% (matching the explanation verbatim), and emphasizes a latex-free environment / latex elimination and product substitution as prevention. Citation is genuine and well-matched.

stem: A 33-year-old woman at 36 weeks gestation is brought to the operating room for a non-obstetric appendectomy under general anesthesia. After preoxygenation she is positioned flat and supine on the OR table while lines are placed. Over the next several minutes her blood pressure drops from 124/76 to 84/48 mmHg, she becomes lightheaded and nauseated, and the fetal heart rate tracing shows new late decelerations. Which intervention is most appropriate to address the underlying cause?

choices.text: Administer a 250 mL intravenous crystalloid bolus and recheck the pressure in 10 minutes
choices.rationale: A fluid bolus may transiently raise pressure but does not relieve the mechanical caval compression driving the hypotension and fetal distress.

choices.text: Apply left uterine displacement by tilting the patient or manually displacing the gravid uterus to the left
choices.rationale: Left uterine displacement relieves compression of the inferior vena cava and aorta by the gravid uterus, restoring venous return and correcting both the hypotension and the fetal late decelerations.

choices.text: Place the patient in steep Trendelenburg to augment venous return
choices.rationale: Trendelenburg does not relieve the uterus-on-vena-cava compression and can worsen ventilation and aspiration risk in a term parturient.

choices.text: Give a 100 mcg IV bolus of phenylephrine to restore systemic vascular resistance
choices.rationale: A vasopressor may treat the symptom but ignores the reversible mechanical obstruction and does not relieve the impaired uteroplacental perfusion.

choices.text: Increase the inspired oxygen concentration to 100% and add 5 cm H2O of PEEP
choices.rationale: Increasing FiO2 addresses oxygenation but not the caval compression responsible for the maternal hypotension and fetal decelerations.

explanation: Beyond about 20 weeks gestation, the supine position allows the gravid uterus to compress the inferior vena cava and aorta, producing aortocaval compression (supine hypotensive) syndrome with reduced venous return, maternal hypotension, and impaired uteroplacental perfusion manifesting as fetal late decelerations. The definitive first step is to relieve the mechanical compression with left uterine displacement, achieved by left lateral tilt of about 15-30 degrees (commonly via a wedge under the right hip) or manual leftward displacement of the uterus. Fluids and vasopressors are adjuncts but do not correct the underlying obstruction.
subtopic: Patient positioning and aortocaval compression
reference_title: Aortocaval Compression Syndrome - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The vignette (>20 weeks gestation, supine/flat positioning, progressive maternal hypotension with lightheadedness/nausea, and new fetal late decelerations) is the textbook presentation of aortocaval compression (supine hypotensive) syndrome. The keyed answer—left uterine displacement via lateral tilt or manual displacement—is the single best intervention because it directly relieves the mechanical IVC/aortic compression driving both the hypotension and impaired uteroplacental perfusion. All distractors are genuinely inferior: (A) crystalloid bolus is only an adjunct and the "recheck in 10 minutes" is unacceptable with active fetal distress; (C) steep Trendelenburg does not relieve caval compression and worsens aspiration/ventilation risk in a term parturient; (D) phenylephrine treats the symptom (SVR) but not the obstruction and does not restore uteroplacental flow; (E) increasing FiO2/PEEP addresses oxygenation, not the mechanism. Per-choice rationales and the explanation (compression beyond ~20 weeks, 15-30 degree left tilt/wedge or manual displacement, fluids/vasopressors as adjuncts) are accurate and current. The well-known nuance that 15 degrees of tilt may incompletely relieve compression does not undermine the key, since the answer accepts greater tilt and manual displacement. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430759/ resolved successfully to the StatPearls "Aortocaval Compression Syndrome" chapter, which directly covers the syndrome, the gravid uterus compressing the IVC and aorta after ~20 weeks, the clinical features, and left lateral positioning/right hip elevation/15-30 degree tilt/manual uterine displacement as management. Citation is valid and precisely on-topic.

stem: A 58-year-old man with a cerebellar meningioma is undergoing a suboccipital craniotomy in the sitting position under general anesthesia maintained with sevoflurane, fentanyl, and 60% nitrous oxide in oxygen. About 90 minutes into the resection, the precordial Doppler suddenly emits a loud churning, mill-wheel sound. The end-tidal CO2 abruptly falls from 34 to 19 mmHg, SpO2 drops to 90%, and the blood pressure decreases from 118/70 to 84/52 mmHg. Which of the following is the most appropriate IMMEDIATE next step?

choices.text: Notify the surgeon to flood the field with saline and discontinue nitrous oxide while delivering 100% oxygen
choices.rationale: Correct: stopping further air entrainment (flooding the field) and discontinuing nitrous oxide while giving 100% oxygen directly addresses the cause and prevents expansion of entrained air.

choices.text: Place the patient immediately in the Trendelenburg, head-down supine position before any other intervention
choices.rationale: Incorrect: although a head-down/left-lateral position is theoretically helpful, it is impractical and dangerous to reposition an open cranium first, and it does not stop ongoing air entrainment the way field flooding and stopping N2O do.

choices.text: Increase the inspired nitrous oxide concentration to deepen anesthesia and blunt the hemodynamic response
choices.rationale: Incorrect: nitrous oxide diffuses into and enlarges the air embolus, worsening the obstruction, so it must be stopped, not increased.

choices.text: Apply 10 cm H2O of PEEP to raise central venous pressure and seal the venous entry points
choices.rationale: Incorrect: PEEP is of limited and debated benefit, can paradoxically promote paradoxical air embolism through a patent foramen ovale, and is not the immediate priority over halting entrainment.

choices.text: Administer a 1 mg/kg bolus of lidocaine to suppress the dysrhythmia generating the abnormal Doppler sound
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the mill-wheel murmur reflects intracardiac air, not a primary dysrhythmia, so an antiarrhythmic does nothing to treat the embolism.

explanation: The sitting position for posterior fossa surgery carries a high risk of venous air embolism because the surgical site sits above the level of the heart, creating a negative pressure gradient that entrains air through noncollapsible dural venous channels. A sudden drop in end-tidal CO2 with a mill-wheel murmur on precordial Doppler is the classic presentation. The immediate priorities are to stop further air entry (alert the surgeon to flood the field, lower the head, apply bone wax) and to deliver 100% oxygen while discontinuing nitrous oxide, which would otherwise expand the entrained gas and worsen the obstruction.
subtopic: Venous air embolism in the sitting/seated position (posterior fossa neurosurgery)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The stem is a textbook VAE presentation in the sitting position for posterior fossa surgery: sudden mill-wheel murmur on precordial Doppler, abrupt ETCO2 fall (34→19), SpO2 drop, and hypotension — all internally consistent. The keyed answer (A) pairs the two recognized immediate priorities: alerting the surgeon to flood the field (halt air entrainment) and discontinuing nitrous oxide while delivering 100% O2 (prevent embolus expansion and improve oxygenation). All distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly explained: C (increase N2O) directly expands the embolus; E (lidocaine) wrongly treats a dysrhythmia when the mill-wheel reflects intracardiac air; D (PEEP) is of debated benefit and risks paradoxical embolism via PFO; B (reposition open cranium head-down 'before any other intervention') is impractical/dangerous as the first step and does not stop entrainment. No distractor rivals A, so the answer is the single best choice. The only nit is minor imprecision in B's rationale (it references a head-down/left-lateral Durant maneuver while the choice text says head-down supine), but this does not affect the keyed logic or correctness. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/venous-air-embolism/ resolved successfully and is squarely on-topic — it covers precordial Doppler, the mill-wheel murmur, ETCO2 fall, high-risk sitting posterior fossa procedures, and the exact management steps (notify surgeon to flood field, discontinue N2O, 100% O2). Both clinical and reference are sound; keep.

stem: A 24-year-old man sustained a complete C6 spinal cord injury 10 days ago in a motorcycle crash. He is now scheduled for urgent laparotomy after a fall produced a splenic laceration. He is hemodynamically borderline with a heart rate of 52/min and blood pressure of 92/54 mmHg, and a rapid-sequence induction is planned. Regarding neuromuscular blockade for intubation, which of the following is the most appropriate management?

choices.text: Use rocuronium for rapid-sequence intubation because succinylcholine risks lethal hyperkalemia at this stage after injury
choices.rationale: Correct: by roughly 24-72 hours after denervation, upregulation of extrajunctional acetylcholine receptors makes succinylcholine capable of causing massive potassium efflux and cardiac arrest, so a nondepolarizer such as rocuronium is preferred.

choices.text: Use succinylcholine because the proliferation of extrajunctional receptors has not yet developed at 10 days
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the receptor upregulation that makes succinylcholine dangerous begins within 24-48 hours and is well established by 10 days, the period of greatest hyperkalemia risk.

choices.text: Avoid all neuromuscular blockers and perform an awake fiberoptic intubation without paralysis
choices.rationale: Incorrect: while awake fiberoptic intubation is reasonable for an unstable cervical spine, it is not mandated solely to avoid paralysis, and a nondepolarizer can be used safely if relaxation is desired.

choices.text: Use succinylcholine but pretreat with a defasciculating dose of a nondepolarizer to prevent the potassium rise
choices.rationale: Incorrect: defasciculation does not reliably prevent the receptor-mediated hyperkalemic response seen after denervation injury.

choices.text: Use succinylcholine at half the usual dose to limit the magnitude of potassium release
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the hyperkalemic response is not reliably dose-dependent in upregulated muscle, and even reduced doses have produced fatal arrest.

explanation: After an acute spinal cord injury, denervation triggers proliferation of immature extrajunctional (fetal-type) acetylcholine receptors across the muscle membrane. When succinylcholine depolarizes these widespread receptors, a massive efflux of potassium can produce hyperkalemic cardiac arrest. This vulnerability begins within about 24-72 hours of injury and may persist for months, so succinylcholine should be avoided and a nondepolarizing agent such as rocuronium used instead. The patient's bradycardia and hypotension are also consistent with neurogenic shock from loss of sympathetic outflow above T6.
subtopic: Acute spinal cord injury — succinylcholine hyperkalemia and timing

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer (A — use rocuronium, avoid succinylcholine) is unambiguously the single best answer. At 10 days post-C6 SCI, denervation-induced upregulation of extrajunctional (fetal-type) ACh receptors is well established, so succinylcholine risks lethal hyperkalemic arrest and a nondepolarizer is preferred for the planned RSI. All distractors are genuinely inferior: B is factually wrong (receptors fully upregulated by 10 days); C (awake FOI to avoid paralysis) is not mandated since a nondepolarizer is safe; D (defasciculation does not prevent denervation hyperkalemia — correct); E (response not reliably dose-dependent, fatal arrest reported with reduced doses — correct). Stem physiology is accurate: C6 is above T6, so the bradycardia/hypotension are correctly attributed to neurogenic shock from loss of sympathetic outflow. Minor nit: the rationale/explanation state onset 'within ~24-72 hours,' which is slightly looser than the commonly cited 48 (or 48-72) hour figure, but this is within accepted teaching ranges and is immaterial because 10 days is well past any threshold — it does not create ambiguity. REFERENCE: https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/spinal-cord-injury/ resolves (HTTP 200) and is directly on-topic; it explicitly states 'Succinylcholine is contraindicated 48 hours after a severe SCI due to concerns for hyperkalemia' and recommends nondepolarizing alternatives, fully supporting the keyed answer. Both dimensions sound; keep.

stem: A 71-year-old woman with hypertension and a 90% symptomatic left internal carotid stenosis is undergoing carotid endarterectomy under an ultrasound-guided cervical plexus block with the patient awake. Her baseline blood pressure is 150/82 mmHg. Shortly after the surgeon applies the carotid cross-clamp, she becomes confused, slurs her speech, and develops decreased grip strength in the right hand. Her SpO2 is 99% and end-tidal CO2 is normal by nasal sampling. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Convert to general anesthesia immediately and reverse the cervical block to abort the neurologic deficit
choices.rationale: Incorrect: emergent conversion to general anesthesia abandons the very monitoring that detected the deficit and does nothing to restore perfusion to the ischemic hemisphere.

choices.text: Administer a vasodilator to lower blood pressure and reduce cerebral metabolic demand during clamping
choices.rationale: Incorrect: lowering blood pressure would further reduce collateral cerebral perfusion pressure and worsen the ischemia.

choices.text: Reassure the patient and continue observation, as transient confusion is expected with cross-clamping
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a new lateralizing deficit during clamping signals inadequate collateral flow and demands intervention, not observation.

choices.text: Alert the surgeon to place an intraluminal shunt and raise the blood pressure toward or above baseline to augment collateral flow
choices.rationale: Correct: in the awake patient, a new neurologic deficit after clamping indicates inadequate cerebral collateral perfusion, and the management is to place a shunt and support blood pressure at or above baseline to maximize flow through the circle of Willis.

choices.text: Give a 250 mL crystalloid fluid bolus and recheck the examination in five minutes before notifying the surgeon
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a delay of several minutes during active hemispheric ischemia risks permanent infarction, and a modest fluid bolus does not address the clamp-induced flow deficit.

explanation: The principal advantage of performing carotid endarterectomy under regional anesthesia with the patient awake is real-time neurologic monitoring, which is considered the gold standard for assessing the need to shunt. A new contralateral motor or speech deficit appearing after cross-clamp application indicates that native collateral flow through the circle of Willis is insufficient. The correct response is to alert the surgeon to place an intraluminal shunt and to raise the systemic blood pressure to or modestly above baseline to augment collateral cerebral perfusion until flow is restored.
subtopic: Carotid endarterectomy under regional anesthesia — awake neuromonitoring and shunt decision
reference_title: Carotid endarterectomy using regional anesthesia: technique and considerations (Frontiers in Surgery)

verdict.reason:
CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Awake CEA under cervical plexus block is the gold standard for the shunt decision because of real-time neuro monitoring. With a 90% symptomatic LEFT ICA stenosis, the new RIGHT-hand weakness and slurred speech after cross-clamping correctly lateralize to ischemia in the clamped left hemisphere, indicating inadequate collateral flow through the circle of Willis. The keyed answer (D: alert surgeon to place an intraluminal shunt and raise BP to/above baseline) is the single best response. All distractors are genuinely inferior: A (convert to GA) abandons the monitoring that detected the deficit and restores no perfusion; B (vasodilator/lower BP) worsens collateral hypoperfusion; C (observe) ignores a new lateralizing deficit; E (fluid bolus + 5-min delay) dangerously delays treatment of active hemispheric ischemia and does not address the clamp-induced flow deficit. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current.

REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11187481/ resolves. Page is 'Carotid endarterectomy using regional anesthesia: technique and considerations,' Frontiers in Surgery (2024) - matches cited title and journal exactly. It is directly on-topic: describes awake CEA under cervical plexus block, intraoperative motor/speech testing, preparedness for 'a smooth transition to shunt should there be changes in the neurological exam,' and BP management ('maintained at the patient's baseline, and may be elevated 10% during ICA clamping'). The citation supports both load-bearing teaching points.

stem: A 29-year-old parturient (G2P1) at 39 weeks with a functioning lumbar labor epidural develops a sudden fetal bradycardia to 70 bpm that does not recover with intrauterine resuscitation, and the obstetrician calls for an emergent (category 1) cesarean delivery. The epidural has been providing a comfortable T10 sensory level, the catheter aspirates negative for blood and CSF, and the airway exam is reassuring. The team elects to extend the existing block rather than perform a spinal or general anesthetic. Which local anesthetic is the most appropriate choice to rapidly establish a surgical level through this catheter?

choices.text: 0.5% bupivacaine, 15 mL
choices.rationale: Bupivacaine has a slow onset (often 15-20 minutes to a dense surgical block), making it too slow for a category 1 emergency despite producing excellent block quality.

choices.text: 3% 2-chloroprocaine, 15-20 mL
choices.rationale: Correct: 2-chloroprocaine has the fastest onset of the neuraxial local anesthetics (dense surgical block in ~3-5 minutes), and its rapid plasma hydrolysis by pseudocholinesterase gives a wide safety margin if intravascular spread occurs.

choices.text: 0.75% ropivacaine, 15 mL
choices.rationale: Ropivacaine, like bupivacaine, has too slow an onset for a true crash cesarean even though it is a sound choice for routine epidural top-up.

choices.text: 1% lidocaine without epinephrine, 20 mL
choices.rationale: Plain lidocaine onsets in roughly 10-15 minutes and provides a less reliable surgical block than alkalinized epinephrine-containing solutions, so it is slower and less dense than 3% chloroprocaine.

choices.text: 0.25% bupivacaine with fentanyl, 15 mL
choices.rationale: This is a dilute labor-analgesia concentration that will not produce a surgical (motor/sensory) block adequate for incision, and the dilute bupivacaine remains slow in onset.

explanation: When a working labor epidural is in place and a true category 1 (crash) cesarean is needed, extending the existing block is often faster and safer than a fresh spinal or induction of general anesthesia. 3% 2-chloroprocaine is the agent of choice because its onset is the fastest of the neuraxial local anesthetics, producing a dense surgical block within a few minutes. Its ester structure is rapidly hydrolyzed by plasma cholinesterase (half-life under a minute), which limits both maternal systemic toxicity and fetal drug exposure. Bupivacaine and ropivacaine, while excellent for elective or non-emergent top-ups, are simply too slow in onset for a fetus with sustained bradycardia.
subtopic: Obstetric — local anesthetic pharmacology: extending a labor epidural for emergent cesarean (2-chloroprocaine)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (3% 2-chloroprocaine, 15-20 mL) is the single best, unambiguous choice for extending a working labor epidural in a category 1 crash cesarean. It is the standard board teaching: fastest-onset neuraxial local anesthetic (dense surgical block in a few minutes), with rapid pseudocholinesterase hydrolysis (half-life ~11-15 sec) limiting maternal toxicity and fetal exposure. All four distractors are genuinely inferior with accurate rationales: 0.5% bupivacaine and 0.75% ropivacaine are correctly too slow; 1% lidocaine WITHOUT epinephrine is correctly slower/less dense than alkalinized epinephrine-containing solutions; 0.25% bupivacaine/fentanyl is a sub-surgical labor-analgesia concentration. The one real-world competitor (2% lidocaine with epinephrine + bicarbonate +/- fentanyl) is deliberately NOT offered, so chloroprocaine wins cleanly with no ambiguity. Explanation is accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532901/ resolves to 'Chloroprocaine - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf,' exactly matching the cited title. The page confirms 3% chloroprocaine as the go-to agent for urgent cesarean, 15-25 mL for a T4 level, and rapid pseudocholinesterase metabolism (maternal half-life 11.2 sec) — fully on-topic and supporting the keyed rationale.

stem: A 31-year-old primigravida at 38 weeks with rheumatic mitral stenosis (echocardiographic valve area 1.1 cm2, mild pulmonary hypertension) presents in early labor requesting analgesia. She is in sinus rhythm at 88 bpm, BP 118/72, SpO2 98%, and is mildly dyspneic with exertion. The obstetric and anesthesia teams plan a vaginal delivery with assisted second stage. Regarding her intrapartum anesthetic and hemodynamic management, which strategy is most appropriate?

choices.text: Withhold neuraxial analgesia and rely on intravenous opioids to avoid any sympathectomy
choices.rationale: Labor pain causes tachycardia and increased cardiac output that are poorly tolerated in mitral stenosis, so avoiding effective neuraxial analgesia worsens her hemodynamics rather than protecting them.

choices.text: Give a rapid 1.5 L crystalloid bolus before placing the epidural to guarantee preload
choices.rationale: Aggressive fluid loading can precipitate pulmonary edema in a fixed, stenotic mitral valve, so large empiric boluses should be avoided in favor of careful, titrated volume management.

choices.text: Treat the expected reflex tachycardia by allowing the heart rate to rise, which improves cardiac output across the stenotic valve
choices.rationale: Tachycardia shortens diastolic filling time across the stenotic valve and reduces left ventricular filling, so a higher heart rate is harmful, not beneficial, in mitral stenosis.

choices.text: Place an early, slowly titrated low-dose epidural to blunt the pain-related tachycardia while maintaining sinus rhythm and avoiding abrupt drops in systemic vascular resistance
choices.rationale: Correct: a slowly titrated epidural controls the painful sympathetic surges that drive tachycardia and preserves heart rate, rhythm, and afterload, which are the core hemodynamic goals in mitral stenosis.

choices.text: Plan a single-shot spinal anesthetic for the delivery to provide rapid, dense analgesia
choices.rationale: A single-shot spinal produces an abrupt sympathectomy with a sudden fall in systemic vascular resistance and venous return that is dangerous in fixed mitral stenosis; a slowly titrated epidural is far safer.

explanation: The hemodynamic goals in mitral stenosis are to maintain a low-normal heart rate (preserve diastolic filling time), maintain sinus rhythm, avoid both hypovolemia and volume overload, and avoid abrupt decreases in systemic vascular resistance. Labor pain provokes tachycardia and increased cardiac output that the fixed valve cannot accommodate, so an early, slowly titrated epidural is the ideal tool: it blunts the painful sympathetic response while allowing gradual, controllable hemodynamic change. Abrupt sympathectomy (single-shot spinal), permissive tachycardia, and large fluid boluses are all poorly tolerated.
subtopic: Obstetric — maternal cardiac disease: hemodynamic management of mitral stenosis in labor
reference_title: Mitral Stenosis: Hemodynamic Management, Comorbidities, and Treatment — OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 3, early slowly titrated low-dose epidural) is the single best answer and reflects current consensus for labor analgesia in mitral stenosis. It is the only option achieving all four hemodynamic goals — low-normal heart rate (preserve diastolic filling), maintenance of sinus rhythm, euvolemia (avoid both hypovolemia and overload), and avoidance of abrupt SVR fall. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and their rationales are accurate: withholding neuraxial permits pain-driven tachycardia/increased CO (harmful); a 1.5 L bolus risks pulmonary edema across a fixed valve; permissive tachycardia shortens diastolic filling time; and a single-shot (local-anesthetic) spinal causes abrupt sympathectomy with sudden SVR/venous-return drop. No two-answer ambiguity — the epidural is unambiguously superior. The explanation is correct and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch confirms the OpenAnesthesia URL resolves (not a 404) and is genuinely on-topic — it states the goals of maintaining normal sinus rhythm, euvolemia/adequate preload, avoiding tachycardia (low-normal HR), heart-rate control for diastolic filling, and normal afterload, exactly the principles tested. The page omits obstetric-specific neuraxial discussion, but the core hemodynamic physiology cited is well represented and appropriate for a keyword-page citation. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 34-year-old parturient with a history of a prior deep vein thrombosis is receiving prophylactic-dose enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously once daily during her pregnancy. She is admitted for induction of labor and requests epidural analgesia. Her most recent enoxaparin dose was administered 8 hours ago; platelet count and coagulation studies are normal. Based on current ASRA/SOAP recommendations, what is the most appropriate management of her neuraxial request?

choices.text: Proceed with epidural placement now, since prophylactic-dose LMWH does not require any delay before neuraxial puncture
choices.rationale: Even prophylactic-dose LMWH requires a defined interval before neuraxial puncture, so placing the epidural at 8 hours violates the recommended waiting period and increases spinal hematoma risk.

choices.text: Reverse the enoxaparin with protamine and then place the epidural immediately
choices.rationale: Protamine only partially reverses LMWH and is not a recommended strategy to shorten the safe interval before neuraxial block, so this approach is inappropriate.

choices.text: Wait until at least 12 hours have elapsed since the last prophylactic enoxaparin dose before placing the epidural
choices.rationale: Correct: ASRA recommends a minimum of 12 hours after a prophylactic LMWH dose before neuraxial needle or catheter placement, which is the prudent interval here.

choices.text: Place the epidural now but delay restarting enoxaparin for 4 hours after the procedure
choices.rationale: The error is in the pre-procedure timing; the safe interval before the block is the issue, and a 4-hour post-procedure restart does not make placement at 8 hours acceptable.

choices.text: Abandon neuraxial analgesia entirely and offer only systemic opioids for the remainder of labor
choices.rationale: Neuraxial analgesia is not contraindicated; it simply needs to be timed correctly relative to the LMWH dose, so categorically denying it is unnecessary.

explanation: ASRA guidelines specify a minimum interval of 12 hours after a prophylactic (low) dose of LMWH and at least 24 hours after a therapeutic (high) dose, such as enoxaparin 1 mg/kg twice daily, before neuraxial needle or catheter placement. After the block, LMWH should be delayed at least 4 hours following catheter removal (and removal should occur at least 10-12 hours after the last LMWH dose). For patients on LMWH longer than 4 days, a platelet count should be checked before the block because of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia risk. Correct timing, not abandonment of neuraxial technique, is the goal.
subtopic: Obstetric — coagulation and neuraxial timing: LMWH thromboprophylaxis and epidural placement (ASRA)
reference_title: Regional Anesthesia in Patients Receiving Antithrombotic or Thrombolytic Therapy: Part 1 — OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 2 — wait until at least 12 hours since the last prophylactic enoxaparin dose) is the single best answer and matches ASRA 4th-edition (2018) guidance: minimum 12h after prophylactic LMWH, 24h after therapeutic LMWH, before neuraxial needle/catheter placement. With the last dose only 8h prior, placement now would violate the interval. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: A falsely claims no delay is needed; B proposes protamine, which only partially reverses LMWH and is not a recommended bridging strategy; D fixates on a correct post-procedure rule (4h restart) but ignores that the 8h pre-block timing is the violation; E needlessly abandons a non-contraindicated technique. The explanation is accurate on the 12/24h intervals, the 4h post-catheter-removal restart, and the platelet-count/HIT check for LMWH >4 days. Only minor looseness: the explanation says removal should occur '10-12 hours after the last LMWH dose,' whereas ASRA specifies >=12h for prophylactic dosing — this is in the explanation, not a choice, and does not affect the keyed answer or distractor logic. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia URL returns HTTP 200; the page title matches the cited title exactly and explicitly states 'Needle placement should occur at least 12 hours after LMWH dose' (prophylactic) and '24 hours' (therapeutic), directly supporting the key. Citation is real and on-topic. Both axes sound; keep.

stem: A 34-year-old woman (BMI 24, no significant comorbidities) presents for outpatient laparoscopic cholecystectomy. She is a lifelong nonsmoker, reports severe motion sickness on cruises, and had vomiting after a prior wisdom-tooth extraction under general anesthesia. The plan is a balanced general anesthetic with sevoflurane and intraoperative fentanyl, and she will likely need oral oxycodone after discharge. Using the simplified Apfel score to guide prophylaxis, which strategy is most appropriate for this patient before emergence?

choices.text: No prophylaxis; treat nausea with ondansetron only if it occurs in the PACU
choices.rationale: She has all four Apfel risk factors (female, nonsmoker, history of PONV/motion sickness, anticipated postoperative opioids), placing her predicted risk near 80%, so withholding prophylaxis is inappropriate.

choices.text: A single agent: dexamethasone 4-8 mg at induction
choices.rationale: Monotherapy reduces baseline risk by only about 25% relative, which is inadequate for a patient with three to four risk factors who warrants combination prophylaxis.

choices.text: Combination of two agents from different classes plus a propofol-based/opioid-sparing technique
choices.rationale: For high-risk patients (3-4 Apfel factors) guidelines recommend two or more prophylactic agents from different mechanistic classes combined with baseline-risk reduction such as a TIVA/multimodal opioid-sparing approach.

choices.text: Two 5-HT3 antagonists (ondansetron plus granisetron) given together for additive effect
choices.rationale: Combining two drugs of the same class (both serotonin antagonists) does not provide additive efficacy and is not a recommended multimodal strategy.

choices.text: Metoclopramide 10 mg as the sole prophylactic agent
choices.rationale: Standard-dose metoclopramide 10 mg has not been shown to be an effective antiemetic for PONV prophylaxis and would be insufficient monotherapy in a high-risk patient.

explanation: The simplified Apfel score counts four factors: female sex, nonsmoking status, history of PONV or motion sickness, and anticipated postoperative opioid use; 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4 factors correspond to roughly 10%, 20%, 40%, 60%, and 80% risk. This patient has all four, so consensus guidelines call for combining two antiemetics from different classes (e.g., dexamethasone plus a 5-HT3 antagonist) AND lowering baseline risk through measures such as propofol TIVA and opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia. Combining drugs of the same class or relying on a single agent is inadequate for high-risk patients in the ambulatory setting where PONV is a leading cause of delayed discharge and unplanned admission.
subtopic: Postoperative nausea and vomiting risk stratification and prophylaxis (Apfel score)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The simplified Apfel score (female, nonsmoker, history of PONV/motion sickness, anticipated postoperative opioids) gives this patient all 4 factors → ~80% risk. The keyed answer (index 2: two antiemetics from different mechanistic classes PLUS baseline-risk reduction via propofol TIVA/opioid-sparing multimodal technique) is the single best choice and matches the 2020 Fourth Consensus Guidelines (Gan et al.), which recommend ≥2 interventions for high-risk patients combined with baseline-risk reduction. All distractors are genuinely inferior with accurate rationales: no prophylaxis is inappropriate at ~80% risk; dexamethasone monotherapy is inadequate for 3-4 factor patients (~25% relative reduction per agent); two 5-HT3 antagonists are same-class and non-additive (guidelines explicitly advise against same-class combinations); and standard-dose metoclopramide 10 mg is established as ineffective for PONV prophylaxis (Apfel/Cochrane data). Risk percentages and explanation are correct and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK500029/ resolved successfully to the StatPearls "Postoperative Nausea" chapter, which is squarely on-topic — it covers the high-risk PONV phenotype (young/middle-aged nonsmoking female with PONV/motion sickness history), the ~80% risk at 4 factors, prevention over treatment, and combination/multimodal prophylaxis with multiple drug classes. Citation is valid and relevant. No clinical, reference, or ambiguity issues.

stem: A 27-year-old healthy woman undergoes an ambulatory knee arthroscopy under spinal anesthesia using a 25-gauge Quincke (cutting) needle after two attempts. She is discharged home the same day. On postoperative day 2 she calls the surgery center reporting a bilateral frontal-occipital headache that is severe when she sits or stands and almost completely resolves when she lies flat, accompanied by neck stiffness and mild tinnitus. She is afebrile with no focal neurologic deficits. Which factor most likely contributed to this complication, and what is the most appropriate next step for persistent, disabling symptoms?

choices.text: Use of a cutting (Quincke) needle was a key contributor; offer conservative measures first, and an epidural blood patch if symptoms remain severe and disabling
choices.rationale: Cutting-tip needles raise the incidence of postdural puncture headache versus pencil-point needles, and the definitive treatment for refractory, disabling positional headache is an autologous epidural blood patch after conservative measures fail.

choices.text: Inadequate preoperative hydration caused the headache; mandate 3 L of IV fluid as definitive treatment
choices.rationale: Aggressive hydration has not been shown to prevent or definitively treat postdural puncture headache and does not address the underlying CSF leak.

choices.text: The headache reflects meningitis; obtain emergent lumbar puncture and start broad-spectrum antibiotics
choices.rationale: A clearly positional headache without fever or meningismus is characteristic of CSF leak, not infection, and another dural puncture would be counterproductive.

choices.text: A large-gauge needle would have reduced risk; immediate prophylactic blood patch at first symptom is standard
choices.rationale: Smaller-gauge needles (not larger) lower postdural puncture headache risk, and an immediate prophylactic blood patch before trialing conservative care is not the standard first response.

choices.text: Caffeine withdrawal is the cause; the headache will not improve with any anesthetic-directed therapy
choices.rationale: The strictly postural nature with tinnitus and neck stiffness points to a CSF-leak headache, which does respond to anesthetic-directed therapy such as an epidural blood patch.

explanation: Postdural puncture headache is a classic complication of neuraxial anesthesia in ambulatory patients and is characteristically postural (worse upright, relieved supine), often with neck stiffness and auditory symptoms, presenting within about 5 days. Risk is increased by cutting (Quincke) needles, larger gauge, multiple attempts, and younger female patients; pencil-point (Whitacre/Sprotte) noncutting needles markedly reduce incidence. Initial management is conservative (analgesics, caffeine, recumbency), but for severe, persistent, or disabling symptoms the definitive treatment is an autologous epidural blood patch, which seals the leak and raises intracranial pressure.
subtopic: Post-dural puncture headache after ambulatory neuraxial anesthesia
reference_title: Postdural Puncture Headache - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer A is unambiguously the single best answer. The vignette is a textbook PDPH presentation (young female, cutting/Quincke needle, two attempts, POD2 positional bilateral fronto-occipital headache relieved supine, neck stiffness, tinnitus, afebrile, no focal deficits). A correctly identifies the cutting needle as a key contributor and gives the correct management sequence (conservative first, epidural blood patch for refractory/disabling symptoms). All distractors are genuinely inferior and contain explicit factual errors: B (hydration is not definitive treatment and does not address the CSF leak), C (meningitis is excluded by the positional, afebrile, non-meningismic picture; repeat dural puncture is counterproductive), D (smaller—not larger—gauge reduces risk, and prophylactic blood patch is not standard first response), E (this responds to epidural blood patch, contradicting the claim it won't respond to anesthetic-directed therapy). Rationales and the explanation are accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430925/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Postdural Puncture Headache' chapter, directly on-topic, and confirms every load-bearing claim—cutting needle and gauge as risk factors, pencil-point needles reducing risk, female/young/multiple-attempt risk factors, bilateral fronto-occipital positional headache with neck pain and tinnitus, 48-72h onset, and conservative measures plus epidural blood patch for severe cases. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 52-year-old man (ASA II, well-controlled hypertension) is recovering in Phase II after an ambulatory inguinal hernia repair under general anesthesia with a TAP block. Two hours after surgery, the anesthesiologist assistant assesses him for home discharge using the Post-Anesthesia Discharge Scoring System (PADSS). His vital signs are within 20% of baseline; he ambulates steadily without dizziness; he has no nausea or vomiting; his incisional pain is well controlled on oral analgesics; and his surgical dressing is dry with no bleeding. He has not yet urinated but had a low-risk procedure, voided normally preoperatively, and received minimal IV fluid. Which assessment best reflects current home-readiness criteria?

choices.text: He must demonstrate tolerance of oral fluids and a solid meal before any discharge can be considered
choices.rationale: Mandatory eating before discharge is not a current requirement; routine forced oral intake is not part of contemporary PADSS-based home-readiness criteria.

choices.text: He meets discharge criteria; spontaneous voiding is not mandatory before discharge for a low-risk patient without risk factors for urinary retention
choices.rationale: Modern criteria no longer require universal voiding before discharge, and a low-risk patient meeting all other PADSS domains can be safely discharged with instructions to return if unable to void.

choices.text: He cannot be discharged under any circumstance until he produces a documented void, regardless of his risk profile
choices.rationale: Mandatory voiding for every patient has been abandoned for low-risk individuals because it needlessly prolongs stay without improving safety.

choices.text: He should be readmitted to Phase I recovery because he has not voided
choices.rationale: Failure to void is not an indication to return a stable, alert patient who meets all other recovery criteria to Phase I monitoring.

choices.text: He must achieve a full Aldrete score of 10 in Phase II before the voiding question is even relevant
choices.rationale: The Aldrete score governs Phase I recovery readiness, whereas home-readiness in Phase II is assessed with PADSS, so this conflates the two scoring systems.

explanation: PADSS scores five domains—vital signs, ambulation/mental status, nausea/vomiting, pain, and surgical bleeding—each 0-2, with a total of 9 or more (and no domain critically low) indicating home-readiness; the Aldrete score is instead used for Phase I recovery. Mandatory oral intake and universal voiding are no longer required for all ambulatory patients. For low-risk patients without risk factors for urinary retention (e.g., no spinal/epidural, no pelvic/anorectal surgery, no history of retention), spontaneous voiding is not a prerequisite for discharge, provided clear instructions are given to seek care if they cannot void within a defined window.
subtopic: Ambulatory discharge criteria and home-readiness scoring (PADSS vs. voiding requirements)
reference_title: Aldrete Scoring System - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Keyed answer (index 1) is the single best answer. Current ambulatory discharge guidance (revised PADSS, ASA/ASPAN) does not mandate universal spontaneous voiding for low-risk patients without urinary-retention risk factors; the stem deliberately establishes low risk (GA not neuraxial, inguinal hernia not pelvic/anorectal/urologic, voided preop, minimal IV fluids, no retention history), so such a patient meeting all other PADSS domains can be discharged with return precautions. All distractors are genuinely inferior: forced oral fluids/solid meal (abandoned), mandatory void for every patient (abandoned for low-risk), readmission to Phase I (clinically excessive), and requiring full Aldrete 10 in Phase II (correctly flagged as conflating Aldrete/Phase I with PADSS/Phase II home-readiness). Explanation is accurate and current: PADSS scores five 0-2 domains with >=9 (and no critically low domain) for home-readiness, Aldrete governs Phase I, and forced intake/universal voiding are no longer required. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK594237/ resolves to the correct StatPearls 'Aldrete Scoring System' page, which covers Aldrete and PADSS and explicitly states 'subsequent revisions of PADSS removed the oral fluid intake and voiding before discharge criteria,' directly supporting the keyed answer. On-topic, not fabricated.

stem: An 84-year-old woman with osteoporosis and remote, asymptomatic coronary artery disease (drug-eluting stent placed 5 years ago, currently on aspirin only) is undergoing cemented hemiarthroplasty for a displaced femoral neck fracture under general anesthesia. She is hemodynamically stable with a blood pressure of 128/70 mmHg and SpO2 of 99% on FiO2 0.5. Approximately 60 seconds after the orthopedic surgeon pressurizes the polymethylmethacrylate cement and inserts the femoral prosthesis, her SpO2 falls to 84%, end-tidal CO2 drops from 36 to 22 mmHg, and her blood pressure falls to 70/40 mmHg. Which of the following best explains the most likely underlying mechanism of this deterioration?

choices.text: Embolization of marrow fat and air into the pulmonary circulation, causing acute right ventricular strain and increased dead space
choices.rationale: Correct: Pressurized cementing forces medullary fat, marrow, and air emboli into the pulmonary vasculature, producing pulmonary hypertension, RV strain, and an abrupt fall in end-tidal CO2 from increased dead space — the hallmark of bone cement implantation syndrome.

choices.text: Direct systemic toxicity of absorbed methylmethacrylate monomer causing profound peripheral vasodilation as the sole driver
choices.rationale: Incorrect: Although monomer-induced vasodilation contributes, embolic phenomena from marrow contents are the dominant mechanism, and isolated vasodilation would not produce the sharp drop in end-tidal CO2 seen here.

choices.text: Acute coronary stent thrombosis from perioperative aspirin continuation producing cardiogenic shock
choices.rationale: Incorrect: The tight temporal link to cementing and the falling end-tidal CO2 point to an embolic pulmonary event rather than primary coronary thrombosis, and aspirin was continued appropriately.

choices.text: A transfusion-related acute lung injury reaction to intraoperative blood products
choices.rationale: Incorrect: No transfusion is described, and the deterioration is precisely time-locked to prosthesis cementing rather than to blood product administration.

choices.text: Anaphylaxis to the cephalosporin surgical prophylaxis with secondary bronchospasm
choices.rationale: Incorrect: Anaphylaxis typically follows antibiotic administration on induction rather than appearing exactly at the moment of cement pressurization, and the dead-space picture is more consistent with pulmonary embolism.

explanation: Bone cement implantation syndrome (BCIS) classically presents with hypoxia, hypotension, and a sudden fall in end-tidal CO2 within seconds to minutes of cement pressurization and prosthesis insertion. The leading mechanism is embolization of medullary fat, marrow, and air into the pulmonary circulation, causing acute pulmonary hypertension and right ventricular dysfunction, with monomer-mediated vasodilation and clotting cascade activation as contributors. Elderly patients with femoral neck fractures and pre-existing cardiopulmonary disease are at highest risk, and management is supportive: high FiO2, fluids, and vasopressors with early communication to the surgical team.
subtopic: Bone cement implantation syndrome in cemented hip arthroplasty
reference_title: Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome (WFSA Anaesthesia Tutorial of the Week 351)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: This is a textbook Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome (BCIS) vignette — elderly patient, cemented hemiarthroplasty for femoral neck fracture, abrupt hypoxia + hypotension + sharp EtCO2 fall (36→22) within ~60s of cement pressurization and prosthesis insertion. Keyed answer A (marrow fat/air embolization causing RV strain and increased dead space) is the single best answer and the dominant accepted mechanism of BCIS; the falling EtCO2 from increased alveolar dead space is the hallmark. Distractors are all genuinely inferior: B is correctly defeated by the qualifier "as the sole driver" (monomer vasodilation is only a contributor); C (stent thrombosis) is implausible with a 5-yr-old stent on aspirin and would not cause a dead-space EtCO2 drop or be time-locked to cementing; D (TRALI) has no transfusion in the stem; E (cephalosporin anaphylaxis) is mistimed and does not fit the dead-space picture. Rationales, explanation, and management (high FiO2, fluids, vasopressors, surgeon communication) are accurate and current. Answer is unambiguous. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://resources.wfsahq.org/atotw/bone-cement-implantation-syndrome/ resolves successfully on the WFSA Virtual Library; title "Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome" (ATOTW 351) and abstract confirm it covers BCIS clinical features, pathophysiology, and management — exactly on-topic. Full text is behind login but the citation is valid and matches the question. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 68-year-old man with well-controlled hypertension and type 2 diabetes undergoes total knee arthroplasty under spinal anesthesia. A thigh pneumatic tourniquet is inflated to 300 mmHg for 105 minutes. Immediately after the surgeon deflates the tourniquet, you observe the end-tidal CO2 rise from 34 to 46 mmHg over about one minute, the mean arterial pressure fall from 88 to 62 mmHg, and a few premature ventricular contractions on the monitor. The patient is breathing spontaneously and remains awake and comfortable. What is the most appropriate immediate management?

choices.text: Administer a calcium chloride bolus and hyperventilate to treat presumed severe hyperkalemic cardiac instability
choices.rationale: Incorrect: The transient potassium rise after deflation averages only about 0.3 mmol/L and rarely causes dangerous hyperkalemia, so empiric calcium and aggressive treatment are not warranted for self-limited PVCs.

choices.text: Support blood pressure with fluids and a small vasopressor dose while continuing close monitoring, anticipating spontaneous resolution
choices.rationale: Correct: The washout of accumulated CO2, lactate, and potassium plus reactive hyperemia produces transient hypotension, a rise in end-tidal CO2, and occasional ectopy that typically resolve within about 15 minutes, so supportive management with fluids and judicious vasopressor is appropriate.

choices.text: Immediately re-inflate the tourniquet to halt the metabolic washout and call for cardiology consultation
choices.rationale: Incorrect: Re-inflating after surgical hemostasis is unnecessary and harmful, prolonging limb ischemia for a physiologic, self-limited washout phenomenon.

choices.text: Convert to general anesthesia and intubate to control the rising end-tidal CO2
choices.rationale: Incorrect: The transient rise in end-tidal CO2 reflects washout of accumulated CO2 from the ischemic limb and does not require airway intervention in a comfortable, spontaneously breathing patient.

choices.text: Administer sodium bicarbonate empirically to correct the expected metabolic acidosis before checking a blood gas
choices.rationale: Incorrect: The post-deflation acidosis is mild and transient, and empiric bicarbonate is not indicated without a measured significant acidemia.

explanation: Release of a lower-extremity tourniquet washes out accumulated CO2, lactate, and potassium from the ischemic limb and triggers reactive hyperemia, producing a transient rise in end-tidal CO2, a fall in arterial blood pressure lasting roughly 15 minutes, and sometimes minor ectopy. The mean potassium increase is only about 0.3 mmol/L and lactate peaks near 2 mmol/L, so these derangements are self-limited in most patients. Management is supportive — maintaining perfusion with fluids and small vasopressor doses — rather than aggressive intervention, with attention reserved for patients with poor cardiopulmonary reserve.
subtopic: Physiologic effects of pneumatic tourniquet deflation

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The stem describes textbook physiologic consequences of lower-extremity tourniquet deflation (transient EtCO2 rise, ~15-min hypotension, minor ectopy) in a hemodynamically stable, awake, comfortable, spontaneously breathing patient. The keyed answer (B) — fluids + small vasopressor with anticipation of spontaneous resolution — is the single best, standard-of-care management. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and wrong for distinct, correctly-explained reasons: (A) the K+ rise of ~0.3 mmol/L is far below the threshold for empiric calcium/hyperkalemia treatment; (C) re-inflating after hemostasis is harmful and prolongs ischemia for a self-limited phenomenon; (D) the EtCO2 rise is expected washout needing no airway intervention in a comfortable spontaneously breathing patient; (E) the acidosis is mild/transient and does not warrant empiric bicarbonate. The explanation's quantitative claims are all accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetched successfully — https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/perioperative-tourniquet-use/ resolves to a real, on-topic OpenAnesthesia article ('Perioperative Tourniquet Use,' updated March 2026) that directly corroborates the question's numbers: 'EtCO2 levels peak at 1 minute and return to baseline... in 10 minutes,' 'a 15-minute reduction in CVP and arterial BP,' 'potassium increases by a mean of 0.28 mmol/L,' and 'Lactate increases to a mean peak of 2.13.' Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 72-year-old man undergoes transurethral resection of the prostate under spinal anesthesia with 1.5% glycine irrigation. Roughly 75 minutes into a resection of a large gland, while still awake under spinal block, he becomes restless and nauseated, complains of his vision "going dim," and then has a brief generalized tonic-clonic seizure. His blood pressure is 168/96 mmHg, heart rate 52 beats/min, and SpO2 96%. A stat serum sodium returns at 116 mEq/L. After securing the airway, stopping the resection, and ensuring oxygenation, which intervention is most appropriate?

choices.text: Rapidly correct the sodium with a continuous infusion of 3% saline targeting normalization to 140 mEq/L within 2 hours
choices.rationale: Incorrect: Overly rapid correction risks osmotic demyelination, so the sodium rise must be limited (generally no more than about 8-10 mEq/L in 24 hours) rather than fully normalized within hours.

choices.text: Administer a liter of 0.9% normal saline wide open to dilute the absorbed glycine and restore the sodium
choices.rationale: Incorrect: Isotonic saline does not adequately raise the sodium in severe symptomatic dilutional hyponatremia and adds to the volume overload that accompanies TURP syndrome.

choices.text: Give hypertonic (3%) saline in small aliquots together with a loop diuretic, correcting the sodium in a slow, controlled fashion
choices.rationale: Correct: Severe symptomatic hyponatremia with seizures from irrigant absorption warrants cautious hypertonic saline to raise the sodium, paired with a loop diuretic to offload the excess free water, while keeping the correction rate slow.

choices.text: Restrict all fluids and simply observe, as TURP-related hyponatremia is benign and self-correcting
choices.rationale: Incorrect: Although mild cases respond to restriction and diuresis, a sodium of 116 mEq/L with seizures is life-threatening cerebral edema requiring active treatment with hypertonic saline.

choices.text: Treat the bradycardia and hypertension first with atropine and a beta-blocker before addressing the sodium
choices.rationale: Incorrect: The bradycardia and hypertension reflect raised intracranial pressure from cerebral edema and will improve as the sodium and volume overload are corrected, so the hyponatremia is the priority.

explanation: TURP syndrome results from systemic absorption of hypotonic irrigation fluid through open prostatic venous sinuses, producing dilutional hyponatremia, volume overload, and — with glycine solutions — transient visual disturbance from glycine's role as an inhibitory neurotransmitter. Severe symptomatic hyponatremia (sodium below roughly 120 mEq/L with seizures or coma) is a medical emergency treated with judicious hypertonic 3% saline plus a loop diuretic to remove excess free water. Correction must be slow — generally no more than about 8-10 mEq/L over 24 hours — to avoid osmotic demyelination syndrome.
subtopic: TURP syndrome and dilutional hyponatremia
reference_title: Hyponatremia and Transurethral Resection of Prostate Syndrome (OpenAnesthesia)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound. Stem is a textbook severe TURP syndrome presentation (glycine irrigation, large gland, ~75 min resection, awake spinal patient, restlessness/nausea, transient visual dimming from glycine, GTC seizure, Na 116, plus HTN + reflex bradycardia from cerebral edema). Keyed answer (choice 2: 3% saline in small aliquots + loop diuretic, rate-controlled) is unambiguously the single best option. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and their rationales are accurate: rapid full normalization (0) risks osmotic demyelination; isotonic saline (1) won't raise Na in severe dilutional hyponatremia and worsens TURP volume overload; observation (3) is dangerous at Na 116 with seizures; treating the brady/HTN first (4) misreads a cerebral-edema (Cushing-type) response. The explanation correctly limits 24h correction to ~8-10 mEq/L to avoid ODS. One minor adversarial nitpick: in an ACTIVELY seizing patient, current guidelines favor an initial rapid 3% saline bolus (100-150 mL over 10 min, repeatable) to abort cerebral edema, with only the 24h TOTAL capped — choice 2's blanket 'slow' framing is slightly imprecise. But the loop diuretic correctly addresses the TURP-specific volume overload, the rate-limited-total principle is correct, and this does not create a competing answer or render the key wrong. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia URL resolves to a real article titled exactly 'Hyponatremia and Transurethral Resection of Prostate Syndrome,' directly covering dilutional hyponatremia, glycine absorption, hypertonic 3% saline dosing (incl. the severe/seizure/Na<120 bolus), and ODS/central pontine myelinolysis risk from rapid correction. Citation is genuine and on-topic.

stem: A 64-year-old man with COPD (FEV1 55% predicted, baseline SpO2 93% on room air) and BMI 34 is scheduled for arthroscopic rotator cuff repair. The surgeon requests a single-shot regional technique for postoperative analgesia. An ultrasound-guided interscalene brachial plexus block is performed with 20 mL of 0.5% ropivacaine. Forty minutes later, in the PACU, the patient reports new dyspnea; SpO2 has fallen to 86% on 4 L/min nasal cannula, he is using accessory muscles, and breath sounds are diminished at the right base. A chest radiograph shows an elevated right hemidiaphragm without pneumothorax. What is the most likely explanation for his respiratory distress?

choices.text: Ipsilateral phrenic nerve block causing hemidiaphragmatic paresis
choices.rationale: Correct — the phrenic nerve runs on the anterior scalene just anterior to the brachial plexus, so interscalene block produces near-universal ipsilateral hemidiaphragmatic paresis, which is poorly tolerated in a patient with limited pulmonary reserve and matches the elevated hemidiaphragm on radiograph.

choices.text: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity from the ropivacaine dose
choices.rationale: Incorrect — LAST presents with neurologic (perioral numbness, tinnitus, seizures) and cardiovascular signs, not isolated unilateral dyspnea with a radiographically elevated hemidiaphragm.

choices.text: Tension pneumothorax from needle pleural puncture
choices.rationale: Incorrect — the chest radiograph explicitly shows no pneumothorax, and tension physiology would cause tracheal deviation and hemodynamic collapse rather than an isolated raised hemidiaphragm.

choices.text: Acute bronchospasm from a COPD exacerbation
choices.rationale: Incorrect — bronchospasm produces diffuse wheezing and prolonged expiration, not a unilaterally elevated hemidiaphragm with focally diminished basal breath sounds.

choices.text: Recurrent laryngeal nerve block causing upper-airway obstruction
choices.rationale: Incorrect — recurrent laryngeal nerve block causes hoarseness and potential stridor, not hypoxemia with an elevated hemidiaphragm on imaging.

explanation: Interscalene block reliably spreads to the phrenic nerve, which lies on the anterior surface of the anterior scalene muscle just anterior to the plexus, producing ipsilateral hemidiaphragmatic paresis in nearly 100% of conventional blocks and roughly a 25% reduction in pulmonary function. Healthy patients tolerate this, but those with limited reserve (COPD, obesity) can decompensate, so the technique is relatively contraindicated in severe respiratory disease. Risk is reduced by lowering local-anesthetic volume, injecting more distally/extrafascially, or selecting an alternative (e.g., supraclavicular or suprascapular) approach.
subtopic: Peripheral nerve block complications — interscalene block and phrenic nerve / hemidiaphragmatic paresis

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The keyed answer (ipsilateral phrenic nerve block causing hemidiaphragmatic paresis) is the single best answer. Interscalene block with a conventional 20 mL volume produces ipsilateral phrenic block in ~100% of cases with ~25% reduction in pulmonary function; a COPD/obese patient with limited reserve decompensates. The presentation (dyspnea ~40 min post-block, hypoxemia, accessory muscle use, diminished basal breath sounds, and—critically—an elevated hemidiaphragm on CXR with no pneumothorax) is the classic discriminator. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly excluded: LAST is neurologic/cardiovascular and earlier-onset, not isolated unilateral dyspnea with raised hemidiaphragm; tension pneumothorax is ruled out by the CXR and would cause tracheal deviation/collapse; bronchospasm is diffuse not unilateral; RLN block causes hoarseness/stridor, not an elevated hemidiaphragm. Anatomy, incidence figures, and mitigation strategies in the explanation are accurate and current. (Minor nuance: supraclavicular block is listed as an alternative but itself carries non-trivial phrenic risk; suprascapular is the better phrenic-sparing option. This does not affect the keyed answer or any distractor.) REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK519491/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Interscalene Block' article, directly on-topic. It explicitly states the block is contraindicated in respiratory insufficiency due to high likelihood of ipsilateral phrenic nerve block and diaphragmatic hemiparesis with ~25% reduction in pulmonary function, and lists 'Hemiparalysis of the diaphragm' as a complication—matching the question precisely.

stem: A healthy 29-year-old woman (ASA II) undergoes outpatient knee arthroscopy under spinal anesthesia. To favor rapid recovery and same-day discharge, the anesthetist selects hyperbaric 5% lidocaine. Surgery and the block are uneventful, motor and sensory function recover normally, and she is discharged. About 8 hours later she develops aching, burning pain in both buttocks radiating to the posterior thighs. There is no weakness, no sensory loss, and no bowel or bladder dysfunction; a focused neurologic exam is normal. Which complication best explains her presentation?

choices.text: Cauda equina syndrome
choices.rationale: Incorrect — cauda equina syndrome produces objective deficits such as saddle anesthesia, lower-extremity weakness, and bowel/bladder dysfunction, all of which are absent here.

choices.text: Transient neurologic symptoms (TNS)
choices.rationale: Correct — TNS is a self-limited pain syndrome of the buttocks and thighs appearing within hours of spinal anesthesia with a normal neurologic exam, and spinal lidocaine carries the highest risk.

choices.text: Post-dural-puncture headache
choices.rationale: Incorrect — PDPH is a positional headache, not a bilateral buttock-and-thigh pain syndrome, and does not match this presentation.

choices.text: Anterior spinal artery syndrome
choices.rationale: Incorrect — anterior spinal artery syndrome causes motor paralysis with loss of pain/temperature sensation below the lesion, which contradicts the normal exam.

choices.text: Epidural hematoma compressing the conus
choices.rationale: Incorrect — an epidural hematoma causes progressive objective neurologic deficit and back pain requiring urgent imaging and decompression, not isolated pain with a normal exam.

explanation: Transient neurologic symptoms (TNS) is a pain-only syndrome of the buttocks and posterior thighs that begins within a few hours to a day after spinal anesthesia and resolves over several days, with a normal neurologic, imaging, and electrophysiologic workup — distinguishing it from cauda equina syndrome. Spinal lidocaine carries by far the highest incidence (about 4-fold relative to bupivacaine, prilocaine, or procaine), and mepivacaine is similar; 2-chloroprocaine is a reasonable short-acting alternative that does not appreciably cause TNS. Management is supportive with NSAIDs.
subtopic: Neuraxial local anesthetic selection — transient neurologic symptoms after spinal lidocaine

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Key (TNS) is the single best answer and unambiguous. The stem is the textbook TNS presentation: a pain-only syndrome of bilateral buttocks radiating to posterior thighs, onset ~8 h after hyperbaric 5% spinal lidocaine, with a completely normal neurologic exam and no bowel/bladder dysfunction. Lidocaine is the highest-risk agent (~4-fold RR). All distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly excluded by the normal exam: cauda equina syndrome (requires saddle anesthesia, weakness, bowel/bladder deficits), PDPH (positional headache, not buttock/thigh pain), anterior spinal artery syndrome (motor paralysis + dissociated sensory loss), and epidural hematoma (progressive objective deficit + back pain). Explanation is accurate and current — TNS resolves over days with normal imaging/electrophysiology, ~4-fold RR vs bupivacaine/prilocaine/procaine, mepivacaine similar, preservative-free 2-chloroprocaine a reasonable short-acting alternative with negligible TNS, supportive NSAID management. REFERENCE: WebFetched https://www.openanesthesia.org/local_anesthetic_transient_neurologic_symptoms/ — resolves (HTTP 200), directly on-topic, and corroborates the specifics including TNS as exclusively a pain syndrome of buttocks/thighs with normal neuro exam, onset within hours lasting up to ~10 days, and lidocaine RR ~4.35. No clinical, reference, or ambiguity problems found.

stem: A 58-year-old woman undergoes an open right hemicolectomy for colon cancer under general anesthesia. As part of a multimodal, opioid-sparing plan, the team places bilateral ultrasound-guided transversus abdominis plane (TAP) blocks at the end of the case, depositing local anesthetic between the internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscles. In the PACU she is comfortable when lying still but reports significant deep, cramping, poorly localized periumbilical pain that worsens with bowel manipulation and is not relieved by the block. Which statement best explains the limitation of the TAP block in this patient?

choices.text: The block failed because local anesthetic was deposited in the wrong fascial plane
choices.rationale: Incorrect — the anesthetic was placed correctly between internal oblique and transversus abdominis, and her incisional (somatic) comfort indicates a technically successful block.

choices.text: The TAP block covers only the anterolateral abdominal wall and provides somatic, not visceral, analgesia
